name: 288 GTO 1984-1987
description: The Ferrari 288 GTO, officially designated as Type F114 and produced between 1984 and 1987 at Ferrari's Maranello factory, is a rare homologation special of the Ferrari 308 GTB, designed for Gran Turismo Omologata (homologated). Despite common historical reports, the 288 GTO was not initially conceived for the 1982 Group B Circuit Race series. At the time, Enzo Ferrari did not oversee the Road Car division, which was managed by General Director Eugenio Alzati and FIAT MD Vittorio Ghidella. It was in 1983, amid conversations with friends and clients who noted declining sales due to strong competition and the "excessive gentrification" of Ferrari models, that the idea began to take shape. Turbocharging, which had proved successful in Formula 1, influenced Ferrari to incorporate it into their road cars, especially in light of new tax laws impacting cars above 1999cc. The initial attempt was the 208 Turbo, which lacked an intercooler, leading to performance and reliability issues. Ferrari consulted Nicola Materazzi, a former Osella and Lancia engineer with expertise in forced induction, who joined Ferrari in 1979 after working on the 126 F1 car's turbo experiments. Materazzi endorsed the potential of a 3-liter turbocharged engine capable of delivering 330 bhp, eventually achieving a reliable 400 bhp from 3000cc, or 133 bhp per liter. Based on his reassurance and successful background, Enzo Ferrari trusted Materazzi to deliver on these promises, even half-jokingly suggesting he work on the 268 engine for the Lancia LC2 Group C racer, given its similar displacement and mechanical characteristics.

name: 288 GTO Evoluzione 1986
description: Ferrari created six versions of the 288 GTO Evoluzione, which included five production models and one prototype, to compete in the now-defunct Group B racing series. Introduced in 1986, the Evoluzione was designed to meet the stringent requirements of Group B, with a production run initially planned for 20 cars to comply with homologation standards. However, with the cancellation of Group B, the project was discontinued as the Evoluzione was not suitable for any other racing category. The car featured a highly modified version of the 2.9 L V8 engine from the standard 288 GTO, equipped with twin-turbochargers that enabled it to produce 650 horsepower at 7,800 rpm. Weighing around 940 kg (2,072 lb), the Evoluzione could reach a top speed of 225 mph (362 km/h) and included advanced aerodynamic elements such as front canards, air channels, vents, a large carbon fiber rear spoiler, and several large NACA ducts. These design and mechanical features heavily influenced the development of the iconic F40. Today, all six units of the Evoluzione are believed to still exist, with one retained by Ferrari on display at their engine manufacturing facility in Maranello and another likely used as a prototype for the F40. Notably, several Formula 1 drivers have owned standard 288 GTOs, gifted or offered by Enzo Ferrari himself, including Michele Alboreto, Keke Rosberg, and Niki Lauda, with Lauda receiving the last of the 272 units produced. Michael Schumacher also acquired a 288 GTO in April 1996, which he registered with the number plate S GT 288.

name: F40 1987-1992
description: In the mid-1980s, Nicola Materazzi presented to Enzo Ferrari the idea of showcasing the capability of new high-powered road cars through the Group B racing category, particularly given that such vehicles could no longer safely demonstrate their performance in everyday scenarios. Although Enzo Ferrari was not directly managing production at that time, Materazzi gained approval from General Manager Eugenio Alzati to develop what would become the GTO Evoluzione, albeit only during weekends, leading to a small team working alongside competitors like Porsche’s 959 in FIA Group B. However, the discontinuation of Group B in 1986 left Ferrari with several Evoluzione cars and no competitive category to enter them. Despite this setback, a validation driver persuaded Enzo Ferrari to adapt these cars for road use, aiming to leave a legacy with his final sports car. This led to the development of the F40, a vehicle meant to be purely performance-focused, described by Ferrari’s marketing as a response to customers desiring less luxury and more raw power. Contrasting with Porsche's 959, the F40 was not about pioneering future technologies but rather embracing simplicity and high performance rooted in Materazzi’s extensive racing experience. The design of the F40, led by Pietro Camardella under Pininfarina's Aldo Brovarone, incorporated elements from the 288 GTO Evoluzione, with extensive development of mechanical components to ensure roadworthiness. Enzo Ferrari, eager to see the project completed swiftly, mandated an aggressive timeline that culminated in the unveiling of the F40 in the summer of 1987, reflecting both his and Materazzi’s dedication to creating a straightforward yet formidable sports car.

name: F40 Competizione 1987-1992
description: Michelotto, a Ferrari specialist based in Padua known for their work on the GTO Evoluzione and the road-going F40, prepared three racing chassis for competition, though only two were actively raced. These two cars, with serial numbers 79890 and 79891, saw action as early as 1989, starting in the IMSA series at Laguna Seca Raceway in the GTO category. A notable moment came when Jean Alesi drove an LM evolution model to a third-place finish, behind two Audi 90s and ahead of other factory-backed competitors. However, the car retired after just eighteen laps in the subsequent race. Over the next IMSA season, the F40, driven by various guest drivers including Jean-Pierre Jabouille, Jacques Laffite, and Hurley Haywood, achieved three second places and one third place. Although the F40 did not return to IMSA in 1991, it remained a popular choice among privateers in various domestic GT series, such as JGTC. By 1994, the car entered the international scene, competing in the BPR Global GT Series; one car, driven by Strandell, notably won the 4 Hours of Vallelunga. The following year saw an increase to four competing F40s, developed by Pilot-Aldix Racing and Strandell under the Ferrari Club Italia banner, with a win at the 4 Hours of Anderstorp. Despite another win at Anderstorp in 1996, the F40, outpaced by the new McLaren F1 GTR, faded from competitive GT racing thereafter.

name: F50 1995-1997
description: he Ferrari F50 (Type F130) is a mid-engine sports car. Introduced in 1995, the car is a two-door, two seat targa top. The car is powered by a 4.7 L naturally aspirated Tipo F130B 60-valve V12 engine that was developed from the 3.5 L V12 used in the 1990 Ferrari 641 Formula One car. The car's design is an evolution of the 1989 Ferrari Mythos concept car. The F50's engine predated the car; it was used in the Ferrari 333 SP for the American IMSA GT Championship in 1994, allowing it to become eligible for the stock engine World Sports Car category.

name: F50 GT 1995
description: The Ferrari F50 GT (also known as the Ferrari F50 GT1) is a racing derivative of the Ferrari F50, intended to compete in the BPR Global GT Series against other series rivals, such as the McLaren F1 GTR. After the series folded, Ferrari was unhappy with homologation specials such as the Porsche 911 GT1 being allowed in the newly formed FIA GT Championship and decided to cancel the project due to lack of funding to compete. The car was co-developed with Dallara and Michelotto.

name: Enzo 2002
description: The Ferrari Enzo, officially named the Enzo Ferrari, is a mid-engine sports car developed by Ferrari and introduced in 2002. Named after the company's founder, Enzo Ferrari, the car incorporates cutting-edge Formula One technology, including a carbon-fiber body, F1-style automated-shift manual transmission, and carbon-ceramic disc brakes. It also features advanced active aerodynamics, producing significant downforce to enhance stability at high speeds. The Enzo achieves 343 kilograms of downforce at 200 km/h, increasing to 775 kilograms at 300 km/h, before tapering to 585 kilograms at its top speed. Designed by Ken Okuyama, the head of design at Pininfarina at the time, the Enzo was unveiled at the 2002 Paris Motor Show with a planned production run of 399 units. Ferrari extended invitations to existing customers, particularly those who had purchased the F40 and F50. In 2004, an additional, 400th Enzo was built and donated to the Vatican for charity, later auctioned for $1.1 million. In total, 498 units were produced, including three development mules, which used the bodywork of the Ferrari 348. The Enzo is powered by a 6.0-liter V12 engine, the F140B, which produces 651 horsepower at 7,800 rpm and features a redline of 8,200 rpm. The engine layout is longitudinally mounted with a rear mid-engine, rear-wheel-drive configuration, ensuring optimal weight distribution. The car boasts a state-of-the-art F1 transmission with paddle shifters, capable of shifting gears in 150 milliseconds. This system, though advanced for its time, received some criticism for its abrupt shifting. Performance-wise, the Enzo excels with a 0-100 km/h acceleration time of 3.14 seconds and a top speed of 355 km/h. Its impressive handling is supported by an independent suspension with push-rod-actuated shock absorbers, adjustable from the cabin, and robust Brembo disc brakes. The Enzo rides on 19-inch wheels fitted with Bridgestone Potenza tires, and the car's braking and handling capabilities are further enhanced by its aerodynamic design. In terms of recognition, the Enzo has been lauded as one of the top sports cars of the 2000s by Sports Car International and ranked fourth in Motor Trend Classic's list of the greatest Ferraris of all time. Despite these accolades, its design has been polarizing, with some critics describing it as overly flashy. Nonetheless, the Ferrari Enzo remains a landmark in automotive engineering, celebrated for its performance, technological innovations, and connection to Ferrari's racing heritage.

name: FXX 2005
description: The Ferrari FXX is a high-performance race car introduced by Ferrari in 2005, based on the street-legal Enzo. Designed for track use only, the FXX incorporates advanced technology from the Enzo and further innovations from Ferrari and its suppliers. Owners participate in exclusive track events and receive detailed performance briefings from Ferrari. The car also shares some technology with the Maserati MC12 Corsa, developed by Ferrari's sister company. The FXX's engine, derived from the Enzo's, has been enlarged to 6.3 liters, producing 800 horsepower at 8,500 rpm. The car's gearbox, incorporating Ferrari's latest F1 technology, shifts gears in under 100 milliseconds. It retains the Enzo's carbon-ceramic brake discs but features upgraded brake pads and custom-developed 19-inch slick tires. Comprehensive telemetry systems allow drivers to enhance their performance while providing Ferrari with valuable data to refine the FXX and future models. Ferrari produced 30 FXX units, with the first 29 sold to selected customers and the 30th given to F1 World Champion Michael Schumacher upon his retirement in 2006. Schumacher's unique FXX features black paint without a stripe, red-trimmed wheels, matte exhaust tips, and his personal logo on the racing seats. Owners of the FXX participate in Ferrari's testing and development programs, with their cars maintained by Ferrari. The program grants exclusive access to Ferrari's latest technology and incorporates customer feedback into the development of future models. The FXX features a longitudinal, rear-mounted 6.3-liter V12 engine, producing 790 horsepower and 506 lb-ft of torque. The car's construction includes a carbon fiber body over a carbon fiber tub with a rear alloy subframe. It has Brembo carbon-ceramic disc brakes, 19-inch wheels, and a sophisticated suspension system with adaptive dampers and electronic shock absorbers. The FXX can reach a top speed of 391 km/h. Ferrari continued to refine the FXX through the Evolution kit, which improved power output to 860 horsepower at 9,500 rpm and reduced gear shift times to 60 milliseconds. These enhancements, along with aerodynamic improvements and an upgraded traction control system, aimed to keep the FXX at the forefront of automotive performance. In popular culture, Michael Schumacher's black FXX appeared on the BBC's Top Gear, where it set a lap record of 1 minute 10.7 seconds. However, the record was later removed because the car used slick racing tires, which are not street-legal.

name: P4/5 by Pininfarina 2006
description: The Ferrari P4/5, officially named the Ferrari P4/5 by Pininfarina, is a one-off sports car created by Pininfarina for American film director James Glickenhaus. Based on the Ferrari Enzo, the P4/5 was redesigned to evoke the styling of Ferrari's 1960s race cars, particularly the P Series. This project, costing Glickenhaus approximately $4 million, was unveiled in August 2006 at the Pebble Beach Concours d'Elegance. The development of the P4/5 began in March 2005 when Glickenhaus was approached by Pininfarina with the idea of commissioning a unique car. The design process started with sketches by Jason Castriota and involved extensive computer-aided design and wind tunnel testing. The result was a car with over 200 custom components, all fitting perfectly due to precise virtual modeling. The P4/5 maintained the Enzo’s engine and drivetrain but featured an entirely new carbon-fiber body. The P4/5 is powered by a Ferrari F140 B V12 engine, similar to that in the Enzo, producing 660 horsepower at 7,800 rpm. The car retains the Enzo’s six-speed automated manual transmission, but with improved aerodynamics, the P4/5 achieves a top speed of 375 km/h and accelerates from 0 to 100 km/h in 3.0 seconds. Its design includes elements from various historic Ferrari models, such as the Ferrari 512S rear window and the Ferrari 333 SP nose, enhancing both aesthetics and performance. The interior of the P4/5 was customized to Glickenhaus’s specifications, including an iPod nano stereo, a tablet PC for navigation and car diagnostics, and custom-molded seats tailored for Glickenhaus and his son. Pininfarina also improved the car’s air conditioning and rearranged the wiring for easier servicing. The P4/5’s carbon-fiber construction and meticulous design resulted in a car that is both lighter and more aerodynamic than the Enzo. Despite initial secrecy, the P4/5 was eventually recognized by Ferrari, and it was officially badged as a Ferrari. This collaboration highlighted Pininfarina’s capability to create bespoke vehicles that honor Ferrari’s legacy while pushing design boundaries. The P4/5 remains a unique testament to the artistry and engineering prowess of both Pininfarina and Ferrari.

name: MC12 2004-2005
description: The Maserati MC12 is a high-performance grand tourer designed for both road and track use, marking Maserati's return to international racing after a 37-year hiatus. Developed with input from the Ferrari Maserati Group, the MC12 benefits from cutting-edge technology and aerodynamic styling. Designed by Giugiaro with significant contributions from Frank Stephenson, the car combines elegance with aerodynamic efficiency. Its design and development emphasize performance, making it capable of exceeding 330 km/h and accelerating from 0 to 100 km/h in just 3.8 seconds. The MC12's design is a blend of racing functionality and aesthetic appeal. It features a carbon fiber body and a stress-bearing chassis made from a carbon fiber and Nomex honeycomb sandwich, supported by aluminum sub-chassis. This construction ensures structural rigidity while keeping the car's weight down. The car's aerodynamic elements, such as the large rear wing, rear diffusers, and engine snorkel, have been meticulously developed to optimize downforce and internal fluid dynamics. The car's bonnet and wheel arches are formed from a single piece of molded carbon fiber, contributing to its sleek and functional design. Under the hood, the MC12 is powered by a naturally aspirated 6.0-liter V12 engine producing 630 horsepower at 7,500 rpm and 652 Nm of torque at 5,500 rpm. This engine is paired with a six-speed Cambiocorsa transmission, which allows for computer-assisted gear selection through paddles behind the steering wheel. The car features two driving modes: Sport, which offers enhanced traction control for regular driving, and Race, which sharpens the car's performance for track use, including faster gear changes and activated ASR. The MC12's interior combines high-tech materials with luxurious touches. The cabin features a removable hard top, allowing the car to convert from a coupe to a spider. The interior design balances simplicity with meticulous craftsmanship, featuring carbon fiber elements, perforated leather trim, and high-grip technical fabric. The dashboard centers around a prominent speedometer, with additional controls for the climate system and other functions placed conveniently on the central console. The seats, made from carbon fiber with high lateral containment, provide comfort and support, while the aluminum pedals and rubber mats underscore the car's sporty nature. In summary, the Maserati MC12 is a blend of advanced engineering and elegant design, capable of delivering exhilarating performance on both road and track. Its development and styling reflect Maserati's racing heritage, while its technological innovations ensure it stands out as a modern high-performance vehicle.

name: MC12 GT1 2004-2005
description: The Maserati MC12's foray into the FIA GT Championship began in 2004 with three GT1 race cars. The AF Corse factory-backed team debuted the MC12 at Imola, but due to debated homologation issues, the FIA did not allow the MC12 to score points. Despite this, the team secured second and third places. At the next round in Oschersleben, Andrea Bertolini and Mika Salo won, marking the MC12's first victory. By the final round at Zhuhai, the FIA homologated the MC12s, allowing them to score points. The MC12 secured another victory, finishing seventh in the teams' championship. In 2005, Maserati dominated the FIA GT Manufacturers Cup, scoring 239 points, nearly double that of their nearest competitor, Ferrari. Vitaphone Racing and JMB Racing, the two teams fielding MC12s, finished first and second in the Team Cup, respectively. Four MC12 drivers were contenders for the FIA GT Drivers' Title at the season's final race, but Gabriele Gardel of Ferrari ultimately clinched the title. The Maserati drivers finished within four points of first place. Vitaphone Racing was Maserati's sole representative in the 2006 season, securing the Teams' Championship despite significant weight penalties. Bertolini and Bartels shared first place in the Drivers' Championship, but the manufacturers' cup went to Aston Martin. In 2007, Vitaphone Racing again won the GT1 Teams' Championship, with Scuderia Playteam and JMB Racing also competing with MC12s. Maserati won the Manufacturers' Cup, and Thomas Biagi claimed the Drivers' Championship. The 2008 season saw Vitaphone Racing return with MC12s for drivers including Andrea Bertolini, Michael Bartels, and newcomer Alexandre Negrão. They won another Teams' Championship and a Drivers' Championship for Bertolini and Bartels. The team also fielded a third car, Team Vitasystem, which competed in one race. JMB Racing participated with a single MC12. In 2009, Vitaphone Racing won their fifth consecutive Team Championship, with Bertolini and Bartels gaining their third Drivers' Championship. The team entered a third car under Vitaphone Racing Team DHL, which finished fourth in the Teams' Championship despite a partial season. With the launch of the FIA GT1 World Championship in 2010, Maserati continued with Vitaphone Racing Team and Triple H Team Hegersport. Vitaphone won five races, securing the Drivers' and Teams' World Championships, though Maserati lost to Aston Martin in the Manufacturers' Trophy. The MC12 also had significant success in the Italian GT Championship. In 2005, Scuderia Playteam and Racing Box entered MC12s, finishing first and third overall. The following year, Playteam won again, and Racing Box came second. However, from 2007, GT1 cars were no longer permitted in the championship, prompting Playteam to move to the FIA GT Championship. Racing Box also won the non-championship 6 Hours of Vallelunga in 2005 and 2006. The MC12's attempt to race in the Super GT series in Japan in 2006 was less successful. Team Goh faced driver issues and disappointing lap times, leading to their withdrawal from the series. In the American Le Mans Series (ALMS), the MC12 faced challenges due to exceeding length and width restrictions. Although modified, it still did not meet all regulations. IMSA allowed the MC12 to compete as a guest in 2005, but it was ineligible to score points and had to carry a weight penalty. Risi Competizione ran the MC12, but with limited success. In 2007, Doran Racing prepared an MC12 for ALMS, achieving a third-place finish at Road America and a class pole at Road Atlanta, though the season was marred by an accident at Petit Le Mans. Despite controversies and regulatory challenges, the Maserati MC12 proved to be a formidable competitor, securing multiple championships and demonstrating Maserati's engineering prowess and resilience in the world of GT racing.

name: MC12 Corse 2004-2005
description: Motorsport forms the core of Maserati's heritage, having spent over thirty years primarily building racing cars, with road cars produced mainly to fund their racing program. However, towards the end of the 1950s, Maserati shifted focus, and the subsequent decades saw the company struggle, culminating in its sale to longtime rival Ferrari in the late 1990s. Under Ferrari's stewardship, Maserati gradually recovered, and in 2004, the company announced a return to top-level racing. Maserati chose to enter GT racing, leveraging the expertise of the Ferrari Maserati Group. With no suitable candidate in Maserati's lineup, they utilized the chassis and drivetrain of the Ferrari Enzo. To meet GT1 class homologation requirements, Maserati planned to produce at least 25 units per year for two years. The resulting car, the MC12, was developed with assistance from Pininfarina, who designed a body optimized for racing. The MC12 differed significantly from the Enzo, featuring a detachable targa roof and a broader, more aerodynamic shape. However, early mistakes nearly derailed the MC12’s racing career. The car exceeded the maximum width specified in regulations, and its racing version was revealed before the road-going model, suggesting it was developed primarily as a race car. This led to resistance from racing authorities, particularly the ACO, which oversees Le Mans. Despite these setbacks, the MC12 was eventually allowed to compete in the FIA GT Championship under specific conditions, including a smaller rear wing and a weight penalty. Maserati's MC12 debuted in the final rounds of the 2004 season, securing two wins and two second-place finishes, proving its competitiveness. In 2005, Maserati expanded its racing efforts to the American Le Mans Series (ALMS), despite continued controversy and protests from the ACO. The MC12 was allowed to compete but was not eligible to score points. Despite this, Maserati participated in all ten rounds, showcasing the brand in the crucial U.S. market. Meanwhile, in Europe, teams like Vitaphone Racing and JMB entered multiple MC12s in the FIA GT Championship. Despite penalties, the MC12s performed well, with Vitaphone Racing winning the Spa 24 Hours and securing the team's championship. The MC12 continued its success in subsequent seasons, with Vitaphone Racing clinching team and driver titles in 2006 and 2007, despite not winning the Spa 24 Hours in the latter year. Stability in regulations allowed Vitaphone Racing to maintain its dominance, winning the constructor's and driver's titles again in 2008 and 2009. In the 2010 season, the MC12 was permitted one final year of competition under the new FIA GT1 Championship regulations. Although it won fewer races, Bertolini and Bartels secured the championship, capping the MC12's illustrious racing career. Despite its success, the Maserati MC12 is often remembered for the controversy surrounding its homologation. The persistent efforts by Maserati to keep the car within regulatory boundaries, despite apparent deviations, suggest a deliberate attempt to push the rules. The MC12's legacy is thus marked by both its racing achievements and the regulatory challenges it faced, illustrating Maserati's determination and resilience in the world of motorsport.

name: Birdcage 75th 2005
description: The Maserati Birdcage 75th is a functional concept car designed and developed by Pininfarina, led by Lowie Vermeersch and including designers Jason Castriota and Giuseppe Randazzo, under the direction of Ken Okuyama. It was first introduced at the 2005 Geneva Auto Show. Named in honor of the classic Maserati Birdcage race cars of the 1960s and Pininfarina's 75th anniversary, the Birdcage 75th resulted from a collaboration between Maserati, Pininfarina, and Motorola to showcase their technical expertise. Remarkably, the car was completed in just two months, following a rapid design phase using 3D renderings. The Birdcage 75th is built on the carbon fiber chassis of the Maserati MC12 GT1 race car and shares many components, most notably the Ferrari/Maserati F140 V12 engine from the MC12 and the Enzo. This mid-mounted engine produces 700 horsepower, contributing to the car's impressive performance capabilities. The exterior bodywork, made entirely of carbon fiber, features diffusers at the rear and two spoilers that automatically raise at speed to enhance aerodynamics. The extensive use of perspex for the windshield and canopy is necessary due to the driver's low-seated position, ensuring optimal visibility. The design of the Birdcage 75th emphasizes both aesthetics and functionality. The front features a low nose with a large grille bearing the Trident emblem, slim LED headlights, and a splitter under the lower air intake. The rear is dominated by a transparent engine cover, showcasing the mid-mounted V12 engine, and a large diffuser with centrally positioned vertical tailpipes. The wheels, measuring 21 inches at the front and 22 inches at the rear, were specially designed for the car. A notable feature of the Birdcage 75th is the absence of traditional doors; instead, a bubble canopy, which includes much of the front bodywork, lifts to allow entry. The interior of the Birdcage 75th, designed with input from Motorola, is a blend of high-tech and luxury. The seats are integrated into the chassis and upholstered in blue suede, complemented by a metal gear shifter and minimalistic controls on the central tunnel. The cabin includes a head-up display (HUD) projected onto a clear panel, showing information typically found on dashboard instruments. The steering wheel features a center-mounted control device similar to a cellphone, and the interior design highlights the tubular frame, reminiscent of the original Birdcage race cars. Rearview cameras replace conventional wing mirrors, further enhancing the car's futuristic appeal. At its unveiling at the 2005 Geneva Motor Show, the Birdcage 75th captured public and critical acclaim, earning the Best Concept award from the show's organizers. It also received accolades such as Automobila Magazine's The Most Beautiful Car in the World (2005) and Louis Vuitton's Classic Concept (2006). Today, the Birdcage 75th is celebrated as one of Pininfarina's masterpieces and a stunning example of automotive design. It remains on display in Turin, Italy, where it can be viewed at Pininfarina's museum. The Birdcage 75th's design philosophy emphasizes a seamless integration of exterior and interior elements, showcasing both the mechanical components and the car's elegant form. The transparent head-up display and the exposed carbon fiber structures reflect a blend of futuristic technology and homage to Maserati's racing heritage. Built entirely of sustainable materials, the Birdcage 75th extends the concept of car/user interface, allowing for enhanced connectivity and an immersive driving experience.

name: MC12 Versione Corse 2006
description: In 2006, Maserati developed a limited-production series called the MC12 Versione Corse, aimed at private customers seeking the ultimate track experience. Derived from the MC12 GT1 racing car, which won the 2005 FIA GT World Endurance Championship, the Versione Corse was designed solely for non-competitive track use. This version was neither road-legal nor constrained by international GT racing regulations, making it a pure track car with exhilarating performance. The naturally aspirated V12 engine of the Versione Corse produced 755 horsepower, more than the Stradale road car and the GT1 race car, as it was not limited by intake air restrictors. With a weight of just over 2,500 pounds, the Versione Corse could accelerate from 0 to 200 km/h in just 6.4 seconds. Only 13 examples of the MC12 Versione Corse were built, consisting of one prototype and 12 customer vehicles. The Maserati MC12 represented a significant chapter in the brand's storied history, harking back to its racing roots from the 1950s when legends like Stirling Moss and Juan Manuel Fangio secured international victories for the marque. During this era, Maserati was also known for producing highly desirable road cars that were fast, sophisticated, and glamorous, although these were secondary to their racing endeavors. By the late 20th century, Maserati had abandoned motorsport to focus on production models. However, at the beginning of the 21st century, Maserati made a dramatic return to its racing roots. Unveiled at the 74th Geneva International Motor Show in 2004, the Tipo M144 or MC12 (Maserati Corse, 12-cylinder) was the most extreme model to leave the Modena factory. Based on the Ferrari Enzo, substantial improvements were made to the engine, chassis, and aerodynamics. Powered by a 5,998 cc, 65-degree, 48-valve, dry-sump V-12 engine, the MC12 developed 630 horsepower at 7,500 rpm, achieving a top speed of 330 km/h. The carbon-fiber body was designed by Maserati Centro Stile, prioritizing function over beauty, bearing the hallmarks of a long-distance racer. The primary purpose of the 50 road-going MC12s produced between 2004 and 2005 was to homologate the model for the FIA GT Championship. Under Giorgio Ascanelli's direction, Maserati's Reparto Corse developed a competition version that achieved numerous international victories, including wins at the Spa 24 Hours. The MC12's prowess in racing led to constructors’, drivers’, and team championships, firmly establishing its reputation. The MC12 Versione Corse, developed in late 2006, catered to wealthy enthusiasts seeking the ultimate track experience. Derived from the competition model, it was conceived as a track car, free from FIA regulations. Without intake restrictors, the Versione Corse's V12 engine produced 755 horsepower, making it the most potent Maserati ever. At 1,150 kilograms, it was almost 200 kilograms lighter than the Stradale. The car lacked traction control, stability control, and ABS, but its performance was electrifying, with the ability to reach 200 km/h from a standstill in 6.4 seconds. The Versione Corse offered full racing car dynamics in a user-friendly package, featuring carbon-ceramic brakes, a six-speed Cambiocorsa gearbox, and an integral roll cage. Only 12 carefully selected customers were able to purchase the MC12 Versione Corse, each at a price of €1,000,000. One of these exclusive vehicles, designated '0008', was delivered to a customer in Germany in 2007. This car, like others in the series, was used sparingly and maintained meticulously, with an engine rebuild by former Maserati Corse mechanics in Modena and maintenance by marque specialist Formula Automobile in Denmark. The MC12 Versione Corse remains a highly coveted collector's item, exemplifying Maserati's racing heritage and engineering prowess.

name: FXX Evolution 2008
description: The Ferrari FXX Evoluzione is an advanced iteration of the original FXX, a track-only prototype car developed by Ferrari and first launched in June 2005. The FXX program, aimed at pushing the boundaries of Ferrari's technology and performance, was extended into 2008/2009 with the Evoluzione package. This update further enhanced the car's handling and performance, incorporating feedback from client test drivers and the invaluable input of seven-time Formula 1 World Champion Michael Schumacher. The FXX Evoluzione boasts a more powerful 6.3-liter V12 engine, which now delivers an impressive 860 horsepower at 9,500 rpm. This upgrade, combined with a revised gearbox capable of shifting gears in just 60 milliseconds, allows the FXX Evoluzione to cut its lap time at Ferrari's Fiorano test track to under 1 minute and 16 seconds. The car's gear ratios were also adjusted to exploit the additional 1,000 rpm provided by the updated engine. Significant improvements were made to the car's traction control system, which now offers nine different settings (plus an off position), allowing drivers to fine-tune the car's behavior on the track to suit their individual driving styles. The new system, developed with Ferrari's GES Racing Division, is less invasive and more adaptable, enhancing both performance and tire longevity. The suspension was modified with new front suspension geometry and a revised setup to complement the car's improved aerodynamics, which now feature a new rear diffuser, nolder, and rear flaps, increasing downforce by 25%. The FXX Evoluzione package includes new 19-inch Bridgestone tires that offer improved durability and Brembo brakes with large Composite Ceramic Material (CCM) discs, which now ensure longer-lasting brake pads. Additionally, the package includes enhanced telemetry systems and the option for two extra video cameras to improve rear visibility without compromising aerodynamics. Ferrari organized a series of track events for FXX Evoluzione owners for the 2008/2009 seasons, with six events each year across North America, Europe, and Asia. These events allow owners to drive their cars on some of the world's most prestigious circuits, supported by a dedicated team of Ferrari engineers, electronics experts, and mechanics. The FXX Evoluzione reflects Ferrari's commitment to advancing automotive technology through rigorous testing and client collaboration. The program not only provides an exclusive driving experience but also contributes to the development of future Ferrari models.

name: Ferrari Millechili 2008
description: Millechili, Italian for one thousand kilograms, is the code name for a prototype sports car to be manufactured by Ferrari. It was a lightweight version of the Enzo Ferrari that would borrow features from Formula One race cars, using the F430's aluminium space frame on a 104.3-inch (2,650 mm) wheelbase. The hybrid power train utilizing a V10 engine used in the car would exceed 602 hp. The car was mainly a technological concept with no intention of production. The Millechili was developed in collaboration with the University of Modena and Reggio Emilia, faculty of Mechanical Engineering. Millechili Lab is a cross-project in which students are working on light-weight car design.

name: GEMBALLA MIG-U1 2010
description: Over the past 27 years, Uwe Gemballa has established a stellar reputation for refining Porsche cars, becoming a well-known figure in the automotive world. Now, the company manager from Leonberg near Stuttgart has embarked on a new venture, transforming the Ferrari Enzo, one of the most potent super sports cars from Maranello, into the GEMBALLA MIG-U1. This unique edition is limited to just 25 individually produced cars, showcasing Gemballa’s expertise and innovative approach to high-performance car modifications. The Ferrari Enzo, introduced over a decade ago, remains a legendary figure in the world of super sports cars. With only 399 examples originally built, most surviving Enzos are now cherished by collectors, making sightings on the road increasingly rare. Among Enzo enthusiasts, the GEMBALLA MIG-U1 represents the ‘Holy Grail.’ This highly exclusive edition, limited to just five cars, is a testament to Gemballa’s ability to push the boundaries of automotive engineering. GEMBALLA’s modifications to the Ferrari Enzo include an increase in the V12 engine's power from 660 to 700 horsepower. The torque is also enhanced to 720 Nm, surpassing that of the ultra-rare, track-only FXX version. These performance enhancements are complemented by an aerodynamic body crafted entirely from carbon fiber. Developed in a wind tunnel, the new body components reduce drag and improve downforce, resulting in better stability at high speeds. The weight reduction, combined with the increased power, allows the MIG-U1 to accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in just 3.1 seconds and achieve a top speed of over 360 km/h. The GEMBALLA MIG-U1 stands out not only for its performance upgrades but also for its bespoke design. Each of the five cars in this limited edition features unique modifications tailored to the individual preferences of its owner. This level of customization, combined with the enhanced performance and striking design, ensures that the MIG-U1 remains a coveted masterpiece among supercar enthusiasts and collectors alike.

name: ZXX 2015
description: The Ferrari Enzo ZXX, the world’s only street-legal Ferrari FXX, has made a triumphant return to ZR Auto in Calgary, Canada. After nearly a year of secrecy, the car has been unveiled with a brand new 6.4L engine installed by Edo Competition, delivering the power and aggressive driving experience that owner Zahir Rana had long desired. The ZXX now pushes over 900 horsepower, making it a true powerhouse on the streets. Rana expressed his satisfaction with the upgrades, noting that the car wasn’t perfect when he first received it back, but now it surpasses his expectations. With the new engine, the ZXX is not only more powerful but also more refined for everyday driving, as Rana envisioned. The car’s return to Calgary coincides with the tail end of the driving season, but Rana wasted no time taking it for spirited drives in the nearby mountains before the snowfall. The ZXX is no ordinary garage queen. Immediately after its return, the team at ZR Auto wrapped the car in Suntek paint protection film to preserve its pristine condition. Rana is known for driving his ZXX hard, and the enhancements made by Edo Competition ensure that it can handle whatever he throws at it. The ZXX’s journey has been remarkable. It was initially rebuilt by Edo Competition after a crash during the 2011 Targa Newfoundland, where it drove off the road and into the Atlantic Ocean. The rebuild transformed the car into the unique street-legal powerhouse it is today. With its new enhancements, the ZXX stands as a testament to high-performance engineering and a passion for pushing automotive boundaries. Next summer, the streets of Calgary will once again echo with the roar of the ZXX, as Rana plans to drive and enjoy his extraordinary car to the fullest. The return of the Enzo ZXX to ZR Auto marks a new chapter in its storied existence, blending everyday usability with track-level performance.

name: LaFerrari 2013-2016
description: LaFerrari, project name F150, is a limited production mid-engine, mild hybrid sports car. LaFerrari means "The Ferrari" in Italian; this is intended to be the definitive Ferrari. LaFerrari is based on findings from testing of the FXX development prototype and on research being conducted by the Millechili Project at the University of Modena. Association with the Millechili Project led to speculation during development that the car would weigh under 1,000 kg, but a dry weight of around 1,255 kg was claimed. Only 499 units were produced, and each cost more than 1 million Euros.

name: LaFerrari Aperta 2016-2018
description: LaFerrari Aperta is the open-top version of LaFerrari. Initially, 200 cars were sold with an additional nine reserved for use during the Ferrari 70th Anniversary celebrations. One more unit was later sold by auction. The Aperta comes with a removable carbon-fiber hardtop and a removable canvas soft top. Other changes include more efficient powertrain's control electronics, re-angled radiators to direct airflow out along the underbody rather than over the bonnet, a longer front air dam to help increase downforce, an L-shaped flap on the upper corner of each windscreen pillar to reduce compression on the rear of the cabin in the absence of a roof, different door opening angles with different wheel arches and a new carbon-fiber insert allowing the doors to rotate.

name: FXX-K 2015-2017
description: The FXX-K is Ferrari's research and development vehicle based on the brand's first hybrid sports car, the LaFerrari. The K in the new car's name refers to the kinetic energy recovery system (KERS) which is used to maximize performance. The FXX-K is not intended to be used in competition and was developed to give an uncompromising experience to the driver.

name: FXX-K Evo 2017-2019
description: The FXX-K Evo was unveiled at the Ferrari Finali Mondiali 2017 on 28 October 2017 at Stazione Leopolda in Florence during the Ferrari 70th Anniversary celebration. It is an aerodynamic package, available for the existing Ferrari FXX-K. The FXX-K Evo allows the car to produce 23% more downforce than the standard FXX-K and 75% more than the road-legal LaFerrari due to a modified front fascia, a large rear wing and underbody diffusers. Performance and weight figures are unknown but a 90 kg (198 lb) decrease in weight is said to estimated by the manufacturer.

name: F80 2024
description: The Ferrari F80 represents the latest in a long line of iconic supercars from the brand, following in the footsteps of the GTO, F40, and LaFerrari. Limited to just 799 units, this model pushes the boundaries of internal combustion engine technology while incorporating the latest hybrid innovations. Designed for maximum performance, the F80 combines an advanced carbon-fiber chassis, extreme aerodynamics, and active suspension to deliver a driving experience that is both track-focused and road-usable. The car’s design and architecture focus on reducing weight, improving aerodynamics, and offering a driver-centric experience that is reminiscent of motorsport, but still accommodating two passengers. At the heart of the F80 is a 3.0-liter turbocharged V6 engine, producing 900 hp, paired with electric motors that add an additional 300 hp, bringing the total output to 1200 hp. This powertrain setup is heavily influenced by Ferrari’s experience in Formula 1 and endurance racing, particularly drawing from the 499P, which recently won Le Mans. For the first time, Ferrari has introduced electric turbo technology (e-turbo), which eliminates turbo lag and provides an instantaneous power response. The hybrid system includes motors on both the front and rear axles, offering four-wheel drive and improved handling through torque vectoring, making it a car that excels both on the road and track. The F80’s aerodynamic profile is designed for optimal performance, with features like an active rear wing, rear diffuser, and advanced underbody work contributing to 1000 kg of downforce at 250 km/h. Active suspension further enhances ground effect and overall stability. The hybrid system also integrates sophisticated regenerative braking, converting energy back into the battery during deceleration. Ferrari’s design approach to the F80 blends aerospace-inspired cues with references to its motorsport lineage, creating a supercar that not only performs at the highest levels but also exudes a bold, aggressive aesthetic. Ferrari has made significant strides in optimizing the car’s engine for both power and efficiency. The combustion chamber has been redesigned for higher pressure and temperature, resulting in enhanced performance. New exhaust and intake systems, along with advanced injection and ignition timing, allow the engine to reach 9000 rpm. The F80 also benefits from state-of-the-art electronics, including a new DC/DC converter that powers multiple vehicle systems with remarkable efficiency. Ferrari's meticulous attention to reducing weight has also extended to the engine, with lightweight materials like titanium and carbon fiber used extensively throughout. The F80's hybrid and electric components are entirely developed in-house, marking a significant evolution in Ferrari’s engineering capabilities. These components, including the electric motors, inverters, and high-voltage battery, are derived directly from motorsport technology and are specifically tailored to maximize performance and minimize weight. The car's battery uses Formula 1-inspired lithium-cell technology, housed in a carbon fiber monocoque case to keep weight low and improve the vehicle's center of gravity. This highly efficient powertrain system not only delivers breathtaking acceleration but also enhances overall driving dynamics, making the F80 a groundbreaking addition to Ferrari’s legendary supercar lineup.

name: Daytona SP3 2023
description: The Ferrari Daytona SP3 is a limited production mid-engine sports car, unveiled on 20 November 2021 for the 2023 model year. The Daytona SP3 is in the "Icona" series of high-performance cars being produced by Ferrari after the Ferrari Monza SP series. The Daytona SP3 is powered by a naturally aspirated 6.5-liter V12 that is shared with the 812 Superfast and marks the first return of Ferrari to naturally aspirated, mid mounted engines for limited edition cars without hybrid electric systems, since the Ferrari Enzo introduced in 2002. The Daytona SP3 is the first road-going Ferrari to feature a mid-mounted 12-cylinder engine without electric hybrid support systems since the Ferrari Enzo in 2002. The engine is mated to a quicker-shifting version of the 812 Competizione's 7-speed automatic dual-clutch gearbox. The SP3 bodywork sits on the LaFerrari Aperta chassis, with carbon fiber used for the shell, chassis and certain bodywork elements. The body work makes use of a removable "Aperta" style roof which can be removed and stored separately. The chassis also consists of butterfly doors which open upwards, similar to the LaFerrari and Enzo.

name: Daytona SP3 ‘Tailor Made’ 2025
description: The Ferrari Daytona SP3 Tailor Made, chassis no. 599+1, is a one-of-a-kind masterpiece crafted for a charity auction at Monterey Car Week 2025, fetching a record-breaking $26 million. This exclusive model, built beyond the sold-out 599-unit run, features a striking two-tone livery of exposed carbon fiber and Giallo Modena, with a unique full-length Ferrari logotype across its body—a first for any Ferrari road car. The interior showcases sustainable Q-Cycle fabric from recycled tires, accented by a Prancing Horse motif, yellow seatbelts, and Formula 1-grade carbon fiber on the dashboard and steering column. A silver plaque marks its unique "599+1" status, with all proceeds benefiting The Ferrari Foundation’s educational initiatives.

name: 330 America 1963-1964
description: Launched in late 1963, the 330 America was a transitional model for Ferrari, sharing the chassis of the outgoing 250 GT/E but equipped with a new engine. This engine was the 4.0 L Tipo 209 V12, which delivered 300 horsepower at 6600 rpm. Visually, the 330 America closely resembled the Series III 250 GT/E, though it occasionally featured an "America" badge on the rear. Like its predecessor, it was fitted with 185VR15 Pirelli Cinturato tires. Only 50 units of the 330 America were produced before it was succeeded by the more substantial 330 GT 2+2.

name: 330 GT 2+2 1964–1967
description: At the 1964 Brussels Motor Show, Ferrari introduced the 330 GT 2+2 as the successor to the 250 GT 2+2. The 330 GT 2+2 featured a 4-liter V12 engine designed by Colombo. However, the most notable aspect of its design was its departure from the traditional Ferrari styling; the 330 GT 2+2 showcased a unique four-headlight front end, a significant shift from Ferrari's typical twin headlamp aesthetic. This model was replaced in 1967 by the Mk II version, which returned to a more conventional two-headlight design, aligning with the classic Ferrari look.

name: 330 GTC (Berlinetta) 1966
description: Introduced at the 1966 Geneva Motor Show, the 330 GTC was designed to bridge the gap between the sporty 275 GTB and the more spacious 330 GT 2+2. It effectively blended the 275 GTB's chassis, which featured all-round independent suspension, with the powerful four-liter engine of the 330 GT 2+2 and the sleek styling of the 275 GTS. The combination resulted in a visually appealing and sharply handling Grand Tourer, continuing the tradition of models like the 250 GT Lusso and the 500 Superfast. Key to the 330 GTC's driving dynamics was the fully independent rear suspension, a significant upgrade over the live axle previously used in Ferrari's Grand Tourers. This, along with the transaxle gearbox, helped achieve an improved weight distribution on its 2400 mm wheelbase chassis. The engine, a long-stroke version of the Colombo SOHC V12, delivered 300 horsepower. It came equipped with three Weber Carburetors and a five-speed gearbox, transmitting power to the rear wheels—a setup that provided an increase in performance over its predecessor, the 330 GT 2+2. Pininfarina, the renowned Turin-based designer and coachbuilder, crafted the bodywork. The design of the 330 GTC was not entirely new but skillfully combined the general shape of the 275 GTS with the front-end design of the 500 Superfast, creating a striking and elegant appearance. Production of the 330 GTC began immediately after its debut and continued until 1968, when it was succeeded by the 365 GTC. This successor maintained the overall design but featured a larger 4.4-liter V12 engine and eliminated the vents behind the front wheels, marking its primary visual differentiation. The 330 GTC model briefly returned in 1971 as the 365 GTC/4, a derivative of the 'Daytona,' but its production concluded after only 500 units due to increasing safety and emissions standards in the United States. This marked the end of the luxury GT model in Ferrari’s lineup just six years after its introduction.

name: 330 GTS (Spider) 1966
description: Ferrari's history with convertibles began significantly with the introduction of the 250 GT Cabriolet in 1957, establishing convertibles as a staple in the Ferrari lineup. Following the end of the Series II 250 GT Cabriolet in 1962, Ferrari launched its successor in 1964, the 275 GTB, which was later adapted into a convertible model named the 275 GTS. The 275 GTS took design cues from its predecessor, the 250 GT Cabriolet, featuring open headlights in contrast to the GTB's covered lights. Underneath, however, it shared much with the 275 GTB, including the all-round independent suspension—a first for a road-going Ferrari, replacing the rear live axle of the 250 GT series. Powered by a 3.3-liter SOHC V12 engine, the 275 GTS offered 260 horsepower. Although it had 20 horsepower less than the Berlinetta, its improved torque at lower revs made it more manageable to drive. The introduction of a rear-mounted five-speed transaxle marked a significant technical advancement. Despite these innovations, the aesthetics of the 275 GTS did not appeal to everyone. The 275 GTS was produced for just two years, with a total of 200 units made, a relatively large number for a Ferrari convertible of that period. It was succeeded by the 330 GTS, featuring a larger engine and a sharper nose design, and later by the 365 GTS in 1969, of which only 20 were produced. These models represented the last of the mass-produced Ferrari convertibles until the launch of the California in the late 2000s.

name: 330 GTC Speciale 1966
description: In the 1950s, most Ferraris delivered to customers for road use featured custom coachwork, directly derived from race cars. It wasn't until the late 1950s that Ferrari introduced its first purely road-going model. These vehicles, often designed by PininFarina, were predominantly commissioned by wealthy clients, including notable figures like King Leopold III of Belgium, whose custom-bodied Ferraris remain highly admired. By the 1960s, Ferrari shifted towards series production of road cars, making custom coachwork less common. However, exceptions persisted, such as the 330 GTC built specifically for King Leopold III's wife, Princesse Lilian de Rethy. Her car, s/n 09439, was her third custom Ferrari, following a Vignale-bodied 250 GT Europa and a PininFarina-bodied 250 GT Coupe. The base for the Princesse's third Ferrari was the 330 GTC, which debuted at the 1965 Geneva Motor Show. In the fall of 1966, two chassis were sent to PininFarina to receive custom bodywork, one of which was for Dr. Michel De Bakey, a close family friend. The design incorporated elements reminiscent of the 365 California but maintained a unique appearance, resembling a coupe version of the last Ferrari built in small numbers. Completed in February 1967, both cars were finished in the same steel blue-grey color. Later that year, two more custom 330 GTCs were created—one with a body similar to the Princesse's and another with a different rear styling. Out of the 604 330 GTCs produced, these four remained the sole custom-bodied examples. Today, chassis 09439 is preserved in its original condition with 26,030 km on the odometer. It was offered for sale for the first time at an Artcurial auction in June 2003. The 330 GTC Coupe Speciale was displayed at the 2003 Techno Classica on the Artcurial stand.

name: 330 GT 2+2 with 500 Superfast body 1967
description: The 1965 Ferrari 500 Superfast Speziale, chassis number 6267GT, is one of the most distinctive and exquisite Ferraris ever owned by HRH Prince Bernhard of the Netherlands, known for his affinity for Ferraris and particularly those painted in his favorite color, green. This specific car stands out among the 37 Ferrari Superfast models produced, mainly due to its unique modifications and distinguished ownership. At the behest of Prince Bernhard, a close friend of Enzo Ferrari and a loyal client, this Superfast was equipped with a 4.0-liter engine instead of the standard 5.0-liter, a modification easily accommodated by the Superfast's chassis that was based on the 330 GT. This particular car features distinctive rear lights and side window frames, adding to its uniqueness. The bodywork, crafted by Pininfarina, was finished in a special metallic color named Verde Pinto, specifically requested by the Prince. Complementing its striking exterior, the interior was luxuriously appointed with stylish beige leather. Powered by a 3.967 cc V12 engine producing 300 horsepower, this Ferrari not only embodies elegance but also significant performance, making it a remarkable example of Ferrari’s bespoke automotive craftsmanship.

name: 330 GT 2+2 Shooting Brake 1967
description: In 1967, Luigi Chinetti Jr., a Ferrari importer, initiated an innovative transformation of a 1965 Ferrari 330 GT 2+2 Series II, chassis number 07963, into a shooting brake. This redesign was a collaborative effort with American illustrator Bob Peak. After finalizing the design, the car was sent to Carrozzeria Vignale in Turin for construction, where it was fitted with engine number 09269. The newly transformed vehicle made its debut on Vignale’s stand at the 50th Torino Motor Show, showcasing a striking metallic green paint with a gold metallic roof. In 2017, the car underwent a repaint, adopting a bronze metallic finish. It was placed on the auction block at Gooding and Co’s 2017 Pebble Beach auction with an estimate of $700,000 to $900,000 but failed to sell. The following year, the car was successfully sold at RM Sotheby's Petersen Museum auction for $313,000, inclusive of the buyer's fee. This unique Ferrari shooting brake exemplifies a bespoke approach to car customization and remains a noteworthy piece of automotive history.

name: Zagato Convertible 1974
description: In 1974, Luigi Chinetti commissioned the creation of a unique Ferrari 330 Convertibile, executed by Zagato, marking their tenth Ferrari project. This model was inspired by the earlier 3Z Spider but incorporated several significant modifications to emphasize its convertible nature, notably featuring a targa-type removable rigid roof. Distinctive changes were made to the car's design to differentiate it from its predecessor. The rear of the convertible was designed to be noticeably higher and bulkier, likely to enhance the structural integrity required for the convertible design. The rear light clusters were also redesigned, set into smaller recesses than those on the original Spider version. At the front, the headlights were covered by darkened plexiglass, adding a modern and sleek touch to its appearance. The car was painted in a light red color, which not only highlighted its robust and slightly heavier build compared to the Spider but also underscored the unique aesthetic Chinetti aimed to achieve for this convertible version. This Ferrari 330 Convertibile stands out as a bespoke creation, showcasing Chinetti's vision and Zagato's craftsmanship.

name: Felber FF 1974-1977
description: In 1974, the Felber FF Ferrari emerged as a unique offering in the market, featuring a 2-door roadster body and crafted under a special arrangement that allowed Swiss specialist Felber to utilize the Ferrari name. This model was based on the underpinnings of the Ferrari 330 GTC and combined classic styling with impressive engineering. The Felber FF was equipped with a 3967 cm3 (242.3 cui) V-12 engine that produced 220.5 kW (300 PS / 296 hp DIN) and 326 Nm (240 lb-ft) of torque, paired with a 5-speed manual transmission. This powerful setup enabled the Felber FF to achieve strong performance metrics: it could accelerate from 0-100 km/h in approximately 4.6 seconds, reach 200 km/h in 17.9 seconds, and complete a quarter mile in 12.7 seconds. Dimensionally, the Felber FF measured 3950 mm (155.5 inches) in length, 1650 mm (65 inches) in width (excluding mirrors), and 1180 mm (46.5 inches) in height, providing a sleek and low profile typical of high-performance roadsters. From 1974 to 1977, only six or seven examples of the Felber FF were produced. Each featured hand-made aluminum bodywork crafted by Panther Westwinds, which also assisted in the car’s development. This limited production run and bespoke craftsmanship made the Felber FF a rare and exclusive vehicle, appreciated by collectors and enthusiasts for its distinctive blend of Swiss precision and Italian automotive artistry.

name: 330 LM Barlinetta (LMB) 1963
description: In 1962, in response to changes in racing regulations that favored homologated Grand Turismo cars over the purpose-built sports racers previously used, Ferrari introduced the 250 GTO, which successfully clinched the next three championships and is now revered as one of the finest cars ever built. The Automobile Club de l'Ouest, organizer of the 24 Hours of Le Mans, adapted slower to the changes, creating a special class for prototype racers with a displacement limit of four liters. Although the 250 GTO was competitive, Ferrari sought a more powerful option for an overall win at Le Mans. Leveraging the four-liter engine from the recently launched 400 SuperAmerica, Ferrari developed a new racing car. This engine, based on the Colombo design like the 250 GTO's, was enhanced with dry sump lubrication and modified heads to accommodate six Weber carburetors, boosting its output from 340 to about 390 or 400 horsepower. This engine was mounted on two different chassis types: one following the design of the latest 250 TRs and another using a lengthened 250 GTO chassis. The former was a unique model known as the 330 TRI/LM, and the latter comprised three units named the 330 GTO. The chassis for the 330 GTOs was extended compared to the 250 GTO to accommodate the larger engine, and although all three bore a resemblance to the 250 GTO, they featured unique design elements and a slightly taller engine necessitating a larger bulge on the engine cover. Only two of these cars saw competitive action; one secured second overall and first in class at the Nürburgring 1000 km, and another retired at Le Mans due to overheating. The 330 TRI/LM performed significantly better, achieving Ferrari's third consecutive win at the 24 Hours of Le Mans under Phil Hill and Olivier Gendebien. In 1963, Ferrari continued to develop this concept by equipping four more GT chassis with the 400 horsepower engine, extending the wheelbase to 2500 mm, and fitting a new Pininfarina-designed body crafted by Scaglietti. This design efficiently blended the 250 GTO nose with a rear akin to the 250 GT Lusso and was notably the first to exceed 300 km/h at Le Mans during test days. Although three of the 330 LMBs were entered in the 1963 24 Hours of Le Mans, only one finished, claiming fifth overall and first in class. This marked the end of the competitive phase for these four-liter GTs, which, despite their power, were outclassed by more agile three-liter variants. While not as celebrated as their smaller-engined counterparts, the seven four-liter Ferraris from this era remain highly esteemed in the collector's market and continue to perform well in historic racing events.

name: 330 P4 1967
description: Throughout the 1960s, Ferrari dominated endurance racing, even as the racing world shifted dramatically with John Cooper's introduction of mid-engined F1 racers. Despite being initially surprised in Formula 1, Enzo Ferrari maintained a competitive edge in sports car racing by developing a series of V6 and V12 mid-engined prototypes. Ferrari's exploration into mid-engine sports cars began with the Dino 246 SP in 1961, leading to the V12-engined 250 P. This model spearheaded Ferrari's sports car program and marked a pivotal shift in endurance racing. Following Ferrari's last front-engined victory at Le Mans in 1962, the 250 P scored the first mid-engined win there in 1963, affirming Ferrari's strategy against emerging competition. Across the Atlantic, Henry Ford attempted to acquire Ferrari but after failed negotiations, he aimed to defeat Ferrari on the racetrack. Starting with the Lola Mk 6 based Ford GT, which debuted at the 1964 24 Hours of Le Mans, Ford initially struggled against Ferrari's more reliable 330 P, which led a Ferrari 1-2-3 finish while the Ford GT faced retirements. However, Ford found success in the GT class with the AC Cobra Daytona Coupe. Despite early setbacks, Ford continued to challenge Ferrari with the 7-liter GT40. Ferrari responded with the P2, but both manufacturers faced reliability issues. Ferrari’s only P2 finished 7th at Le Mans, salvaged by a 250 LM win by the NART team. For 1966, Ferrari introduced the P3, adopting fiberglass for lighter doors and a new ZF five-speed gearbox, marking a departure from traditional designs. The P3 initially showed promise with victories at Monza and Spa but faltered at Le Mans due to insufficient preparation time, overshadowed by Ford's dominant 1-2-3 finish. In response, Ferrari developed the P4 for 1967, leveraging a new engine derived from their 3-liter F1 engine. Despite Ferrari's impressive performance at Daytona, where they clinched the top three positions, Ford’s Mk IV outpaced the Ferraris at Le Mans due to superior horsepower. Still, Ferrari won the overall sportscar World Championship that year. Significant rule changes at the end of the season rendered the 330 P3, P4, and 412 P obsolete. Two of the P4s were adapted for Can-Am racing as '350 Can-Am' with a larger engine but struggled against more powerful competition. The legacy of these cars continued as they were sold to privateers, finding success in races around the world for several more seasons. This era marked a significant chapter in racing history, showcasing Ferrari's adaptability and innovation in the face of fierce international competition.

name: 330 P3/4 1966
description: During the late 1950s, Ferrari was caught off guard in Formula 1 by John Cooper's innovative mid-engined racers. However, Enzo Ferrari ensured his dominance in sports car racing remained unchallenged by developing a series of mid-engined V6 and V12 prototypes. This strategic shift began to materialize with the Dino 246 SP in 1961, marking Ferrari's foray into mid-engine sports cars. By 1963, the introduction of the V12-powered 250 P solidified the mid-engine architecture within Ferrari's lineup, proving its merit by securing the first mid-engined victory at Le Mans in 1963. On the other side of the Atlantic, Ford aimed to challenge Ferrari's stronghold in endurance racing. After failed attempts to purchase Ferrari, Ford developed the GT40, making its debut at the 1964 24 Hours of Le Mans. Although the initial models faced reliability issues, the competition intensified with Ford's Cobra Daytona Coupe outperforming Ferrari's GTOs in the GT class. Ferrari responded with the 330 P and the subsequent P2 model, which faced their own reliability challenges, barely making an impact against the formidable Ford GT40. In response to changing regulations and Ford's increasing pace, Ferrari introduced the 330 P3 in 1966, featuring lighter fiberglass doors and a new fuel injection system. The P3 started strong, winning at Monza and Spa, but factory labor issues hampered its development, leading to a disappointing performance at Le Mans, where Ford achieved a commanding 1-2-3 finish. Undeterred, Ferrari continued to innovate with the P4, launched in 1967. The P4 featured a powerful new engine derived from their F1 technology, boasting 450 horsepower. Despite its advancements, the P4 faced fierce competition from Ford's new Mk IV at Le Mans, ultimately placing second. The end of the 1967 season brought regulatory changes that rendered the 330 P3, P4, and 412 P models obsolete, marking an abrupt end to some of Ferrari's most aesthetically appealing and competitive racers. Some P4s were adapted for Can-Am racing but struggled against more powerful and lighter competitors. Despite these challenges, the cars continued to be raced successfully by privateers in subsequent years, underscoring the enduring legacy of Ferrari's innovative spirit in the face of intense international rivalry.

name: 330 P3 1966
description: In the late 1950s, Ferrari found itself unexpectedly challenged in Formula 1 when John Cooper introduced mid-engined racers. While caught off guard in F1, Enzo Ferrari ensured dominance in sports car racing by developing a series of mid-engined V6 and V12 prototypes, starting with the Dino 246 SP in 1961. This initiative evolved into the V12-engined 250 P, which became a cornerstone of Ferrari’s sports car program. Ferrari's strategic foresight was validated when their cars, particularly the 250 P, won consecutive races, including the first ever mid-engined victory at Le Mans in 1963. Despite Ferrari’s stronghold on the track, across the Atlantic, Henry Ford attempted to break this dominance by designing the Ford GT. This rivalry intensified when Ford’s negotiations to buy Ferrari failed, leading to the creation of the Ford GT, which debuted at the 1964 24 Hours of Le Mans. The Ford GT initially struggled with reliability, allowing Ferrari’s 330 P, equipped with a four-liter engine, to secure a 1-2-3 finish at Le Mans. However, the competition heated up in the GT class, with Ford achieving success with the AC Cobra Daytona Coupe. In response, Ferrari introduced the P2, which faced its own reliability challenges, barely managing a seventh-place finish at Le Mans, though it was partially redeemed by a 250 LM entered by NART. Responding to regulatory changes in 1966 that improved car speeds by altering windshield widths, Ferrari developed the 330 P3. This model featured lightweight fiberglass doors and a Lucas Fuel Injection system, marking significant departures from previous designs. Despite promising starts at Monza and Spa, labor issues hindered Ferrari’s preparation for Le Mans, where none of the P3s completed the race. Conversely, Ford achieved a stunning 1-2-3 victory at the event. Determined not to be outpaced, Ferrari developed the P4 for the 1967 season, upgrading the engine to 450 horsepower with new features enhancing its efficiency. Despite the improvements, Ford’s Mk IV, with its powerful seven-liter engine, dominated at Le Mans, surpassing Ferrari’s sophisticated P4s. Rule changes later that year rendered the 330 P3, P4, and 412 P models obsolete, cutting short the careers of some of Ferrari’s most visually appealing and competitive racers. Some P4s were adapted for the Can-Am series but faced challenges against more powerful competition. The legacy of these vehicles continued through privateers who achieved continued success in races globally, showcasing the enduring spirit and innovation of Ferrari in the face of fierce rivalry.

name: 330 P2 1965
description: In 1965, Ferrari introduced the 330 P2, a significant evolution designed to meet the intensifying competition from Ford and its GT models. Developed as part of the World Sportscar Championship, the 330 P2 featured a semi-monocoque spaceframe chassis that incorporated stressed aluminum panels to create a lighter yet more rigid structure. This was complemented by a cutting-edge suspension design inspired by Formula 1, featuring unequal length wishbones at the front and a complex rear linkage system with inboard ventilated Dunlop disc brakes, enhancing the car's agility and handling. The aerodynamics of the 330 P2, crafted by Fantuzzi, focused on minimizing drag and maximizing downforce. It boasted a longer nose and a truncated tail with an integrated spoiler, optimizing its performance for high-speed endurance racing. Under the hood, the 330 P2 was equipped with a Colombo-derived 60° V12 engine, available in 3.3-liter and 4.0-liter variants. These engines were enhanced with dual overhead camshafts, a departure from the previous single cam configurations, and a sophisticated fuel system with twin plug ignition, enabling up to 410 horsepower in the 4-liter version. The car's racing debut underscored its prowess. Participating in events such as the 1000 km of Monza and the 24 Hours of Le Mans, the 330 P2 demonstrated competitive performance against formidable rivals, showcasing Ferrari’s engineering expertise and racing heritage. Throughout the 1965 season, it not only achieved notable results but also set the stage for future advancements in Ferrari’s racing strategy, maintaining its legacy in the motorsport world.

name: 330 P 1964
description: Enzo Ferrari's approach to racing car development was characterized by a preference for evolutionary rather than revolutionary changes. This conservative strategy focused on reliability and incremental improvement, leading to consistent success in motorsports. Despite the shift toward mid-engine configurations by many manufacturers in the early 1960s, Ferrari maintained a traditional setup for their sports racers until 1963, by which time they had already secured three consecutive 24 Hours of Le Mans victories, proving that their cautious approach was not disadvantageous. In 1963, Ferrari introduced their first sports racer with a mid-mounted V12 engine, leveraging insights from previous seasons' six and eight-cylinder models. The car featured a straightforward yet effective steel tubular spaceframe and used double wishbones with coil springs for the suspension. It also employed Dunlop disc brakes with inboard rear mounts to reduce unsprung weight and enhance handling. The traditional space for the V12 engine was instead utilized for the radiator and fuel tanks. The engine, a continuation of Gioachino Colombo's original V12 design from 1946, was now positioned behind the driver. Modified to the latest '250' specifications and equipped with six Weber Carburetors, it generated just over 300 horsepower. Named the 250 P, the car was adorned with a sleek aluminium body designed by Pininfarina and built by Fantuzzi, featuring aerodynamic elements like an airfoil behind the cockpit and a cut-down rear to minimize drag, reminiscent of the aero features on the front-engined 330 TRI/LM. Weighing just 760 kg, the 250 P was significantly lighter than its front-engined predecessor, the 250 TR. It debuted internationally at the Sebring 12 Hours, where Ferrari dominated with a one-two finish, kicking off a successful season that included wins at the Nürburgring 1000 km and the 24 Hours of Le Mans. At Le Mans, Lorenzo Bandini and Ludovico Scarfiotti clinched the first victory for a mid-engined car in the race’s storied history. Later that year, Ferrari unveiled the 250 LM, a production version of the 250 P. Sticking to their usual practice of gradual improvement, Ferrari then introduced evolved versions of the 250 P. The reworked bodies featured altered A-pillars and a longer tail, with engine options expanded to include the 3.3 liter '275' and the 4 liter '330' variants, producing 320 and 370 horsepower, respectively. Despite the availability of these more powerful engines, it was a 275-engined 250 P that achieved a crucial victory at Le Mans, outperforming the larger 330 variant. As Ford’s racing program began to gain momentum with the GT40, Ferrari responded by developing the P2, a new prototype intended to continue Ferrari’s competitive edge into 1965 and beyond, marking the next chapter in Ferrari’s storied racing legacy.

name: 365 California 1966–1967
description: The 365 GT4 BB was unveiled at the 1973 Paris Motor Show during the onset of the oil crisis, which initially dampened demand. Despite a slow start with only about two dozen cars produced in the first year, Ferrari's production eventually increased to one car per day. By the end of its production run in 1976, a total of 387 units had been made, making it the rarest of the Berlinetta Boxer models. Among these, 88 were right-hand drive, with 58 designated for the UK market. The bodywork was crafted by Scaglietti with assembly completed at Ferrari's Maranello factory. The car received a positive reaction from contemporary press for its handling and straight-line speed. Road & Track's June 1975 testing, despite a slipping clutch, recorded acceleration times with a top speed of 282 km/h, making it the fastest road car they had tested up to that point. A later Car and Driver test in November 1976 showed improved acceleration times, emphasizing the vehicle's performance capabilities. Optional features included air conditioning, which was popular among buyers, and a choice between the two-tone "Boxer" paint scheme and a single color finish.

description: The 365 GT4 BB was unveiled at the 1973 Paris Motor Show during the onset of the oil crisis, which initially dampened demand. name: BB 512 1976-1981
description: The BB 512, introduced in 1976 as the successor to the 365 GT4 BB, featured significant enhancements and modifications. The name 512 signified its 5-liter, 12-cylinder engine, marking a departure from the traditional Ferrari naming convention that focused on individual cylinder displacement. The BB 512 debuted at the 1976 Paris Motor Show. The engine of the BB 512 was enlarged to 4943 cc with a bore and stroke of 82 mm x 78 mm and an increased compression ratio of 9.2:1. Originally claimed to deliver 360 horsepower, this figure was later adjusted to 340 horsepower. Although this was slightly lower than its predecessor, the BB 512 achieved peak power at 6200 rpm, which was lower than the 7000 rpm peak of the 365 GT4 BB. The design changes facilitated compliance with stricter pollution and noise regulations while maintaining performance levels. The BB 512 incorporated a dual plate clutch that eased pedal effort and featured dry sump lubrication to prevent oil starvation during hard cornering. To enhance grip, rear tire width was increased to 225 70 VR 15, and the rear wheels were widened to 9 inches. The front tires remained the same size as those on the 365 GT4 BB, at 215 70 VR 15. Despite an unchanged chassis, the wider rear tires resulted in a broader rear track, and the rear bodywork was widened to accommodate this. Externally, the BB 512 was distinguishable by a new chin spoiler integrated into the bumper, which eliminated the nose-lift experienced at high speeds in the previous model. Additional cooling for the exhaust system and rear brakes was provided by NACA ducts positioned behind the doors. The rear of the car featured twin tail lights and exhaust pipes on each side, contrasting with the triple units of its predecessor, and the engine cover now included more vents to improve cooling.

name: BB 512i 1981-1984
description: Introduced in 1981, the BB 512i, also known as the 512 BBi, marked the conclusion of the series. This model incorporated the Bosch K-Jetronic CIS fuel injection into the Tipo F110 A engine, which improved emissions and provided a better balance of performance and driveability. Changes also included updated camshaft timing and the introduction of the Magneti Marelli "Dinoplex" electronic ignition system with a built-in rev limiter. The BB 512i was rated at 340 horsepower at 6,000 rpm, making it the least powerful model in the series. Externally, the BB 512i could be distinguished from the BB 512 by features such as small white running lights on the front fascia, a front grill that showcased the driving lamps, and a revised rear valence that included red fog lamps adjacent to the exhaust pipes. The venting on the engine cover was also modified. The vehicle transitioned to using Michelin TRX metric-sized tires and wheels, featuring 240/55 VR 415 tires on both front and rear, with wheel sizes of 180 TR 415 front and 210 TR 415 rear. Standard features included air conditioning, power windows, and a stereo, enhancing the car’s luxury offerings. An exclusive option was an interior trim with Ermenegildo Zegna wool cloth on the seats, door panels, and headliner, a feature believed to be included in only 27 examples.

name: 365 GT4 BB 1974
description: The 365 GT4 BB, though not officially raced by Scuderia Ferrari, saw competitive action through modifications made by Luigi Chinetti's North American Racing Team (NART). NART acquired two units of the 365 GT4 BB, chassis 18139 and 18095, and adapted them for sports car racing. Chassis 18139 underwent significant modifications, including the removal of the standard interior for a roll cage, a larger fuel tank, enhanced suspension, widened bodywork with fixed headlights, and broader wheels. While the engine remained largely unaltered, minor adjustments were made to the carburetor and a new exhaust system was installed. Despite its extensive preparations, 18139 faced mechanical failures, retiring from several races including the 1975 24 Hours of Daytona due to a hub carrier break. However, it achieved a notable 6th place at the 12 Hours of Sebring that same year. In 1977, the car finished 16th overall and 5th in the IMSA class at the 24 Hours of Le Mans. For the 1978 season, the car was upgraded with a 512 engine from Ferrari, participating in various races and concluding its competitive appearances in 1984 at the 6 Hours of Riverside, where it was no longer competitive and retired after 76 laps.
