Chassis 18095 received less extensive modifications, including wider wheels, flared wheel arches, a small rear spoiler, and racing-specific safety features such as a roll bar, harness, and fire extinguisher. Its engine remained standard apart from a spacer for increased oil capacity and a new exhaust system. This car was scheduled to race in the 1975 24 Hours of Le Mans but was withdrawn by NART as a protest against the race organizers' classification decisions.

name: 512 BB LM 1978
description: In 1978, Ferrari launched the 512 BB LM, a purpose-built competition version of the BB 512, produced in two series until 1982. The initial series in 1978 comprised three cars, featuring wider wheel arches, a roof-mounted aerofoil, and a rear wing derived from a Ferrari 312T2. These series one cars were lighter at approximately 1,200 kg and boasted a power increase to between 400 and 440 horsepower. Two of these cars had "long nose" bodywork, extending the front and introducing a smaller air intake instead of the standard grill. These three factory series one 512 BB LMs participated in the 1978 24 Hours of Le Mans, supported by entries from Ferrari importer Charles Pozzi and NART. Additionally, Jacques Swaters' Ecurie Francorchamps entered a 512 BB modified for competition, which was heavier by about 100 kg. None of these cars completed the race due to mechanical failures. Recognizing the shortcomings of the first series, Ferrari initiated a second development program in late 1978. This series replaced the carburetors with a Lucas mechanical fuel injection system, boosting power to approximately 470-480 horsepower. The cars also received a new body design from Pininfarina, which was longer and wider, slightly increasing the weight but incorporating fixed headlights and an extended tail. Improvements were also made to the transmission and cooling systems, brakes, and suspension. By 1979, nine series two 512 BB LMs were constructed. Further enhancements in 1980 included the introduction of "ground effect" side skirts and a larger air inlet duct, alongside a lighter chassis with fiberglass body panels. This reduced the weight by an additional 100 kg. A total of 25 series two 512 BB LMs were built from 1980 to 1982. The 512 BB LM, never raced by Scuderia Ferrari itself, was used in competition by various independent teams with limited factory support. These cars appeared in World Endurance Championship and IMSA races, achieving notable finishes such as fifth overall and first in the GTX class at the 1981 24 Hours of Le Mans. Despite their potential, reliability issues and inconsistent factory backing limited their success. After retiring from professional racing, the 512 BB LMs have continued to appear in vintage racing events globally.

name: Testarossa 1984-1991
description: The Testarossa is a two-door coupé that premiered at the 1984 Paris Auto Show.[3][4] All versions of the Testarossa were available with a rear-mounted, five-speed manual transmission. The rear mid-engine design (engine between the axles but behind the cabin) keeps the centre of gravity in the middle of the car, which increases stability and improves the car's cornering ability, and thus results in a standing weight distribution of 40% front: 60% rear

name: Testarossa Spider 1986
description: The Testarossa Spider (s/n 62897) is the sole official convertible variant of the Testarossa commissioned in 1986 by the then-Fiat chairman Gianni Agnelli to commemorate his 20 years of chairmanship of the company. The Testarossa Spider has an Argento Nürburgring exterior, a white magnolia leather interior with a dark blue stripe running above the matte black sills, and a white electronically operated soft top that could be manually stowed away. The vehicle was delivered to Agnelli in four months, and had a solid silver Ferrari logo on the hood instead of an aluminium one. The silver theme refers to elemental silver's periodic table abbreviation "Ag", the first two letters of Agnelli's name. Despite many requests from interested customers for a Testarossa Spider, Ferrari refused to produce the car as a regular production variant of the Testarossa, citing spatial and structural challenges that would be difficult to resolve, and so Pininfarina and some aftermarket firms such as Straman, Pavesi, Lorentz and Rankl, and Koenig Specials offered unofficial Spider conversions on special consumer requests. The official Spider is no different mechanically from the normal Testarossa available on the European market, though the top speed was reduced because of the excessive weight arising due to the reinforced chassis. It has a special transmission manufactured by Valeo installed which is convertible to both automatic and the standard 5-speed manual versions with the push of a button, a technology ahead of its time. The transmission was installed on special request of Agnelli as he suffered from a chronic leg injury.

name: F90 1988
description: For almost 18 years, Ferrari denied that the F90 existed. The project was eventually discovered along with the fact that six were made for the Sultan of Brunei in 1988. The project was managed by Enrico Fumia, the head of the Research and Development department at Pininfarina. At the time, the project was top secret to that extent that Ferrari themselves didn't know of the project. Fumia styled the car and said the F90 name referred to it being a "Ferrari of the '90s." All six F90s used a Ferrari Testarossa chassis on top of which Pininfarina sculpted an entirely new body and interior. The engines were stock units, having a power output of 385 hp and having a rear-wheel drive layout, but the radiators were moved to the front of the car.

name: Mythos 1989
description: The Ferrari Mythos is a mid-engine, rear wheel drive concept car based on the mechanical underpinnings of the Ferrari Testarossa. Designed by Italian design house Pininfarina and developed by automobile manufacturer Ferrari, its world premiere was at the 1989 Tokyo Motor Show. The design was implemented on the platform of the Ferrari Testarossa, which dictated the car's wedge shape and large air intake ahead of the rear wheels. The design of the Mythos later evolved into the Ferrari F40's successor, the F50. The show car is stored at the Pininfarina style center at Cambiano (Italy).

name: Colani Ferrari Testa d'Oro 1989
description: Designed by Luigi Colani in 1989, the Testa d'Oro was designed to break land speed records at the salt flats. It was based on a Testarossa with a turbocharged flat-12 engine featuring a 5.0 L Ferrari-Lotec turbocharger. The engine had a power output of 750 hp. It successfully broke the record in its class in 1991, reaching 351 km/h with catalytic converters fitted.

name: FX 1996
description: The Ferrari FX was a special order sports car custom made by Pininfarina at the request of the 29th Sultan of Brunei. It featured the flat-twelve engine of the Ferrari F512 M, on which it is based, and a 7-speed sequential manual transmission from the Williams Formula One team. Only seven or nine cars were made depending on the source, six of which were delivered to the royal family in Brunei. After the Sultan cancelled delivery of car number four, Dick Marconi bought the car from Williams. Car number four is now on display at the Marconi Automotive Museum in Tustin, California, US. The FX has a top speed of around 205 mph, yet Ferrari claimed "We don't want to make the fastest car in the world".

name: FZ93 1993
description: The FZ93 (Formula Zagato '93) was designed by Ercole Spada as a follow-up to Zagato's series of Ferrari specials. In 1994 the car was reworked and repainted all-red; it was also renamed as ES1 in honour of its designer.

name: 512 TR 1992-1994
description: The 512 TR sports a 4.9-litre longitudinally rear-mounted Tipo F113 D flat-12 engine. Each cylinder of the engine has four valves, with forty-eight valves total. These combine to a maximum power output of 422 hp. The car can accelerate from 0–100 km/h in 4.8 seconds. It can complete a standing quarter mile in 13.2 seconds. The 512 TR has a top speed of 313 km/h.

name: F512 M 1995-1996
description: The F512 M was the last version of the Testarossa. 501 cars were produced in total, of which 75 were made for the North America market. It was first shown at the Paris Salon in October 1994 and the last car was built in early 1996. All United States models sold (none were sold new to Canada) were model year 1995.

name: 550 Maranello 1996-2002
description: The Ferrari 550 Maranello (tipo F133) is a front-engine V12 2-seat grand tourer built by Ferrari from 1996 to 2002. The 550 Maranello marked Ferrari's return to a front-engine, rear-wheel drive layout for its 2-seater 12-cylinder model, 23 years after the 365 GTB/4 Daytona had been replaced by the mid-engined Berlinetta Boxer.

name: 550 Maranello World Speed Record (WSR) 1998
description: In October 1998, Ferrari sent a lightly modified 550 to a 12 kilometers oval test track to the Transportation Research Center in East Liberty, Ohio and set three new world records as follows: A distance of 100 miles covered at an average speed of 190.2 mph (306 km/h). A distance of 100 kilometers at an average speed of 188.9 mph (304 km/h). Driving for one hour at an average speed of 184 mph (296 km/h). To celebrate these records, Ferrari built 33 road cars to the exact specification of the record-setting car. Aside from the actual record setting car, all the WSR cars were finished to European specifications. These differ from the standard 550M by having been fitted with the Fiorano handling pack, a leather-trimmed roll cage, suede-covered steering wheel, and carbon bucket seats with race harnesses and "Daytona" stitching. A WSR plaque was installed on the center console and carbon fiber detailing was applied to the gear knob and footrests.

name: 550 Barchetta Pininfarina 1998
description: The Ferrari 550 Barchetta Pininfarina, introduced at the 2000 Paris Motor Show, commemorated the 70th anniversary of Pininfarina. This limited-edition roadster was designed as a true barchetta, without a traditional convertible top. Instead, a cloth soft top was provided, intended only for temporary use to shield the interior from rain, as it was unsafe to use the top above 113 km/h. The Barchetta featured a more steeply raked windshield than the coupe, roll-over hoops behind the seats, and an extended rear section, which provided additional cargo space despite the car being less practical overall. Aesthetically, the Barchetta was distinct with its deeper windshield, bespoke 19-inch alloy wheels, and smoother overall design. The mechanical components were largely similar to the 550 Maranello coupe, but the engine carried the F133C code for differentiation. Despite the loss of the roof, performance remained impressive, with a 0-100 km/h acceleration time of 4.4 seconds and a top speed of 300 km/h. Production was limited to 448 units, a number slightly higher than the initially planned 444 due to superstition in the Japanese market regarding the number four. Each car featured a numbered plaque on the dashboard with Sergio Pininfarina's signature, adding a touch of exclusivity and marking its significance in Ferrari's lineup. The Barchetta Pininfarina remains a celebrated model for its unique design and impressive performance, blending Ferrari's engineering prowess with Pininfarina's distinguished aesthetic sense.

name: Rossa 1998
description: The 550-based Ferrari Rossa was a concept car introduced at the 2000 Turin Motor Show to celebrate the 70th anniversary of Pininfarina. The 2-door speedster shares the mechanical components from the 550 Maranello but its top speed is reduced to 185 mph (298 km/h) due to increased weight. The design of the Rossa was inspired by the 1958 Ferrari 250 Testa Rossa, and the futuristic styling elements later found their way to future Ferrari production cars such as the Enzo Ferrari and the F430. It was designed by Ken Okuyama at Pininfarina.

name: 550 GTS 1998
description: In November 2000, German entrepreneur and engineer Franz Wieth launched another racing version of the 550, developed by Baumgartner Sportwagen Technik, and named 550 GTS. Two cars were built, with Wieth Racing entering one in the 2001 FIA GT Championship, then again in 2003, 2004 and 2005. In 2006 the Wieth's Ferrari scored two wins in the Euro GT Series. Commissioned by Frédéric Dor's company Care Racing Development, in 2001 Prodrive built a racing version of the 550 for various sports car series and especially the 24 Hours of Le Mans. Initially known as 550 GTO and then renamed 550 GTS (but not related to Wieth's project), a total of ten cars would be built over the next four years and campaigned by the Prodrive team as well as privateer customers. The cars were entirely built by Prodrive without any support from the Ferrari factory.

name: 575M Maranello 2002-2005
description: The 575M Maranello represents an upgrade to its predecessor, the 550 Maranello, highlighted by its new V12 engine and the introduction of a first-ever F1 type gearbox for a 12-cylinder Ferrari road car. The V12 maintains its 65-degree V angle, with four overhead camshafts and four valves per cylinder, a light alloy block and heads, an oil tank, and a dry sump lubrication system featuring two scavenge pumps and one sender pump, with separate reservoirs and individual radiators. Performance enhancements were achieved by increasing engine capacity, elevating the compression ratio, and improving the fluid dynamics for intakes, among other efficiency enhancements. The 575M Maranello features a transaxle design ensuring a balanced 50-50 weight distribution between the axles. The rear-mounted gearbox and differential are integrated into a single unit. The electro-hydraulic 'F1' control unit, positioned at the rear, minimizes response time and optimizes operating temperatures. The refined F1 gearchange, operated by manual levers on the steering wheel, supports both sporty and relaxed driving styles. It integrates an analogue control for suspension damping, offering settings for quicker gear changes and adaptive suspension control. The chassis incorporates an adaptive setup with independently controlled damping at each corner, selecting the optimal ride height for any driving condition. The braking system has been enhanced to handle increased performance, focusing on avoiding fade and improving braking response. Stylistically, the 575M Maranello maintains the classic Ferrari front-engine design. Technical modifications include redesigned air intakes, enhanced aerodynamic efficiency, and a new treatment for the front spoiler. The light clusters have been updated to integrate xenon technology for the dipped beam, and the wheels feature a new design. Aerodynamic improvements also reduce drag and enhance airflow around the wheels, while the undercarriage is optimized to generate downforce without increasing drag.

name: 575 GTC 2003
description: Ferrari, a dominant force in GT racing during the 1950s and 1960s, shifted its focus from in-house competition to supporting privateers after the 1966 season. For decades, private teams raced models like the 365 GTB/4 Daytona Competitizione, 512 BB LM, and F40 LM. A significant change occurred at the 2003 Frankfurt Motor Show with the debut of the Ferrari 575 GTC, a GT1 racing version of the 575M Maranello. This development was inspired by the success of the Prodrive-produced 550 Maranello racers, including a class win at Le Mans. The 575 GTC, developed by N-Technology, was based initially on the 550 Maranello. These cars were stripped down and rebuilt with a steel roll-cage and carbon-fibre bodywork. The V12 engine was enlarged to just under six liters and paired with an XTrac six-speed sequential gearbox, producing 600 horsepower within the race's weight limit. Two prototypes were raced by JMB Racing in the 2003 FIA GT Championship, facing reliability challenges throughout the season. The third prototype debuted at the Frankfurt Motor Show and raced to a victory at Estoril by Fabio Babini and Philipp Peter. Despite its initial success, the 575 GTC struggled with speed and reliability, achieving only this single international win. Twelve units were sold, marking it as the last of the V12-engined Ferrari GT racers. An 'Evo' upgrade failed to significantly enhance its competitiveness. The 575 GTC marked a return to Ferrari's involvement in GT racing, setting the stage for subsequent models like the V8-engined F430 GTC. Despite its challenges on the track, the 575 GTC has grown in value and stands as a significant model in Ferrari's racing history.

name: 575 GTC Evoluzione 2005-2006
description: After Ferrari withdrew from all racing except Formula 1 in the early 1970s, customers took to modifying various Ferrari models for competition, achieving mixed success. Notably, the 365 GTB/4 Daytona Competitizione and the 550 GTS Maranello both claimed class victories at Le Mans. The success of the 550 GTS in 2003 at Le Mans inspired Ferrari’s client racing division, 'Corse Clienti,' to develop a race-ready version of their latest Grand Tourer, the 575M Maranello. Independent developers had produced various competition versions of the 550 Maranello, with the Prodrive-prepared examples proving particularly successful, winning their class at Le Mans and in the FIA GT Championship. In response, Ferrari introduced the 575 GTC, offering customers a fully factory-prepared race car, though without any plans to re-enter GT racing as a works team. The 575M Maranello underwent significant modifications for racing in the GTS class. The tubular frame chassis was retained but strengthened with a roll-cage, a wider track was implemented, and extensive use of carbon fiber was made. The engine was enlarged to just under six liters, producing over 600 horsepower with air restrictors. The 575 GTC debuted impressively at the 2003 Estoril FIA GT race, securing a victory and outperforming the Prodrive 550s. However, the car struggled to replicate this success in subsequent races. Despite these challenges, the 575 GTC demonstrated reliability from the start. In the 2004 season, teams continued to fine-tune the 575 GTC to compete effectively against Corvettes and 550 Maranellos. By 2005, Corse Clienti released an updated version of the 575 GTC, aimed at boosting its performance to meet the increasing competition.

name: Superamerica 2005
description: The Ferrari SuperAmerica, designed by Pininfarina, merges the power of a 12-cylinder berlinetta with an innovative convertible design. It introduces a unique rotating roof movement featuring electrochromic technology developed by Saint Gobain, marking it as the first production car with such a feature. The patented electric rotating roof allows the SuperAmerica to convert to open-top in just 10 seconds, achieving a top speed of 320 km/h, making it the fastest convertible berlinetta in the world. Based on the 575M Maranello, the SuperAmerica offers both F1-style and manual gearbox options, with the engine enhanced to produce 540 horsepower. It is also available with the GTC Handling package, which includes advanced features like carbon ceramic brakes. The model’s electric roof, named Revocromico, combines a rearward rotation mechanism with electrochromic technology. The roof’s carbon-fibre structure ensures the boot capacity is unaffected whether the roof is open or closed. The glass rear window doubles as a wind deflector, and the electrochromic glass can adjust the cockpit's light levels, offering five tint settings that change within a minute at the press of a button.

name: 575 GTZ 2006
description: Zagato, traditionally known for crafting bodies ideally suited for competition cars, has rarely worked with Ferrari, mainly producing one-off specials and show cars. A notable exception was a series of five Zagato-bodied 250 GT Coupes created between 1956 and 1959. To mark the 50th anniversary of these cars, a Japanese collector commissioned Zagato to create a modern iteration based on the Ferrari 575M Maranello, named 'Project Zero'. Starting in 2005, this project involved stripping down a 575M Maranello and fitting it with a new aluminum body reflecting design elements from the original 250 GT Zagatos, including the signature 'double bubble' roof, prominent front and rear fenders, and a large grille. Modern updates included integrated front and tail lights in the fenders and a two-tone paint finish. The 'Ferrari 575 GTZ' was unveiled at the Concorso d'Eleganza Villa d'Este, revealing plans to build another five examples, first offered to owners of the original Zagato Ferraris. Not all original owners opted in, but eventually, all new 575 GTZs found buyers, each customized in exterior and interior details. The sixth and final car echoed the first 250 GT Zagato with a similar dark-blue and ivory scheme and added vents behind the front wheels. Given further interest, Zagato also created an open version called the '550 Barchetta Zagato', based on the 550 Barchetta. Due to the additional chassis strengthening required, only three of these were made, including a unique right-hand drive version completed in summer 2010. The 575 GTZ spearheaded a new era of custom Ferraris, leading to an official factory customization program with Zagato as a partner. While these cars didn't match the racing pedigree of their historical counterparts, they served as a significant tribute to the 250 GT Zagato, influencing subsequent Zagato designs.

name: 550 GTZ Barchetta 2009
description: On October 28, 2009, Zagato and Ferrari revealed that they have been working on a roadster version of the 575 GTZ coupé to celebrate the 90th anniversary of the long collaboration between the two Italian establishments. The resulting product called the 550 GTZ Barchetta was limited to only three units and based on the 550 Barchetta Pininfarina, including one RHD example. Unlike the original version, Zagato developed an electronically-operated roof. The design of the GTZ Barchetta was inspired by the 1956 Ferrari 250 GT Berlinetta.

name: Veloce12 Touring 2024
description: The Touring Veloce12, built by Carrozzeria Touring Superleggera, reimagines the Ferrari 550 with a blend of classic Italian coachbuilding aesthetics and modern engineering. This limited-production model uses a lightweight carbon fiber body, designed with CAD precision to integrate seamlessly with the Ferrari 550’s chassis. Advanced aerodynamic studies and load analysis ensure the structure is both strong and efficient, minimizing vibrations and maximizing performance. The Veloce12 retains the Ferrari 550’s 5.5L V12 engine, but with upgrades that bring its output to 503 horsepower, an increase over the original. Paired with an enhanced cooling system and a refined exhaust by Supersprint, the engine offers both thrilling performance and a distinctively powerful sound. The car accelerates from 0 to 100 km/h in just 4.4 seconds, with a top speed of 320 km/h. This impressive power is complemented by a high-performance Brembo braking system, featuring large discs and multi-piston calipers, providing strong and reliable stopping power. Handling is refined through bespoke wheels that reduce unsprung mass and an advanced, adjustable suspension system co-developed with TracTive, allowing drivers to switch between comfort and performance modes. The Ferrari 550’s chassis has also been reinforced to improve torsional stiffness, taking cues from Ferrari’s later models, the 599 and F12, to enhance stability and control. Inside, the Veloce12 showcases Italian craftsmanship with premium materials and hand-stitched leather, creating a luxurious yet purposeful cockpit. Each detail reflects meticulous attention to design, bridging Ferrari’s performance-focused heritage with Touring’s tradition of handcrafted elegance. This fusion of design and engineering earned the Veloce12 the title of Carrozzieri Italiani Car of the Year 2024, highlighting it as a modern icon in Italian automotive artistry.

name: 599 GTB Fiorano 2006
description: The Ferrari 599 GTB Fiorano (internal code F141) is a grand tourer produced by Ferrari. It was the brand's front engined, two-seat model that replaced the 575M Maranello in 2006 as a 2007 model, and was replaced for the 2013 model year by the F12berlinetta. Styled by Pininfarina under the direction of Jason Castriota, the 599 GTB debuted at the Geneva Motor Show in February 2006. The bodywork features optimized aerodynamics with distinct sail panels flanking the rear window, directing and maximizing air flow to a linear rear nolder. The 599 is named for its total engine displacement 5,999 cc (6.0 L), Gran Turismo Berlinetta nature, and the Fiorano Circuit test track used by Ferrari.

name: 599 GTZ Nibbio Zagato 2007
description: The Zagato 599 GTZ Nibbio was introduced in 2007 by Zagato and was based on the Ferrari 599 GTB. It was limited to only nine units worldwide, seven of which were built with the F1 automated manual transmission and two with the 6-speed manual. Two of the ones with the F1 automated manual transmission are spyder. The manual coupe car, painted dark blue with a white top half, was listed for sale in December 2018 for US$1.495 million at Tomini Classics in Dubai.[36] The design of the GTZ Nibbio is inspired by various coach built sports from the 1950's and 1960's.

name: 599 GTB Fiorano HGTE 2010
description: The Ferrari 599 GTB Fiorano HGTE (HGTE being an abbreviation of Handling Gran Turismo Evoluzione) is an upgrade package for the 599 designed to improve the car's handling. It includes a modified set-up with stiffer springs and rear anti-roll bar as well as new calibration settings for the Delphi MagneRide magneto-rheological shock absorbers when the manettino is at its sportier settings. The ride height has also been lowered, resulting in a low centre of gravity. The package also includes optimised tyres featuring a compound that offers improved grip. The car's electronic systems were also changed. The gearbox's shift times were faster in high-performance settings, while new engine software provided improved response. The exhaust was modified to produce a more marked and thrilling sound under hard usage while still delivering the right comfort levels at cruising speed. The exterior and interior were upgraded with more carbon fibre components and the car also included new 20-inch wheels.

name: 599XX 2010
description: At the 2009 Geneva Motor Show,[24] Ferrari unveiled the track-only iteration of the 599 GTB, dubbed the 599XX. Designed by Ferrari's Formula-one engineers, the car has many changes over the standard car in order to make it more nimble and responsive on a race track. Exterior enhancements included two winglets on the C-pillars for improved downforce, a vented bonnet for improved engine cooling, darkened lexan tail lamps, a carbon fibre 'ducktail' rear spoiler aiding further in downforce, a large rear diffuser for improved under body airflow, tow hooks at the front and rear, additional ducts for improved cooling, a minimalist race interior with racing bucket seats along with an LCD display behind the steering wheel replacing all analogue gauges equipped with a roll-cage and lexan sliding windows. The car also has two fans that were located in the trunk and worked to keep the car on the ground and stopped working at speeds up to 249 km/h, a speed at which the car needed no additional downforce.The rev limiter was raised to 9,000 rpm, with the engine rated at 720 hp at 9,000 rpm. A new gearbox was introduced to cut overall gear change time to 60 milliseconds, holding the upward or downward shift paddle for longer resulted in multiple shifts that improved gearing time. At the 2010 Beijing International Auto Show, Ferrari announced that the 599XX had completed the Nordschleife circuit at the Nürburgring in a time of 6 minutes and 58.16 seconds – the fastest time ever recorded for a production-derived sports car. This lap was later beaten by the Pagani Zonda R in June 2010, which had set a lap time of 6 minutes and 47.50 seconds.

name: P540 Superfast Aperta 2010
description: The Ferrari P540 Superfast Aperta is a one-off convertible sports car based on the Ferrari 599 GTB Fiorano. This car represents the second car in Ferrari's Special Project program. Edward Walson commissioned the car in 2008, asking Ferrari to build a modern interpretation of the gold covered Carrozzeria Fantuzzi-bodied Ferrari 330 LMB built for the 1968 film Spirits of the Dead. The P540 was revealed to the public on 11 December 2009, but had been spotted in spy shots several months previous to this. Designed by Pininfarina and built in Maranello, this car was designed to comply with international safety and homologation requirements. Using finite element analysis and carbon fibre, the car's chassis was stiffened for the conversion to a convertible body style. As it is based on the 599 GTB F1, many of the specifications of the P540 are similar to its donor car. Total time of development was 14 months.

name: 599 GTB HY-KERS Prototype 2010
description: Motor Authority reported that Ferrari president Luca Cordero di Montezemolo was working on the development of a Ferrari model that would use alternative energy sources and which would be based on what Ferrari is doing in Formula 1, which uses Kinetic Energy Recovery System. The KERS was tested using a 599 as a test mule. The model was later revealed to be the LaFerrari.

name: 599 GTO 2011
description: On 8 April 2010, Ferrari announced official details of the 599 GTO (for Gran Turismo Omologato). The car was a road-legal version of the 599XX track day car and at the time Ferrari claimed that the 599 GTO was their fastest ever road car, able to lap the Fiorano test circuit in 1 minute 24 seconds, one second faster than the Ferrari Enzo. Its engine generated a power output of 661 hp. The car has the multiple shift program for the gearbox from the 599XX along with the exhaust system. Ferrari claimed that the 599 GTO could accelerate from 0–100 km/h in under 3.3 seconds and has a top speed of over 335 km/h. At 1,605 kg, the 599 GTO weighs almost 100 kg less than the standard GTB. Production was limited to 599 cars. Of these, approximately 125 were produced for the United States market. Ferrari has produced only two other models that used the GTO designation: the 1962 250 GTO and the 1984 288 GTO with the third being the 599 GTO.[23] Unlike the previous GTOs however, the 599 GTO was not designed for homologation in any racing series.

name: 599XX Evoluzione 2011
description: On November 10, 2011, the first images of the 599XX Evoluzione surfaced, which had a radically restyled aero and exhaust package as well as electronic upgrades and Pirelli racing slicks. On 1 December 2011, Ferrari confirmed details for the 599XX Evoluzione in preparation for the 2011 Bologna Motor Show. The 599XX Evoluzione weighed 35 kg less than the standard 599XX and the engine had slightly improved peak power of 730 hp. One of the key features of the Evoluzione is its active rear wing which can adjust automatically to provide increased cornering performance.

name: 599 SA Aperta 2011
description: The roadster variant of the 599, the SA Aperta, was introduced at the 2010 Paris Motor Show as a limited edition in honour of designers Sergio Pininfarina and Andrea Pininfarina, with the "SA" designation standing for both their names. The SA Aperta utilized the higher performance engine and gearbox from the 599 GTO and has a 10 mm lower ride height, thicker rear anti roll bar and recalibrated magnetic dampers. The SA Aperta also has a slightly raked windscreen as compared to the 599 GTB along with larger cooling ducts in the new front bumper and a rear bumper in body colour. The car has a removable soft top that was only intended for use in city driving and to protect the interior from rain. The boot-lid was made of aluminium while the C-pillars were made of carbon fibre and have a silver finish along with the windshield frame. The exhaust system was a modified version of the one used in the 599XX and the car has special 5-spoke wheels unique to the variant with a chromed finish along with a triple layer body paint. The interior was also redesigned and has a two-tone colour scheme and the car has sports seats as standard equipment. Only 80 examples were produced, honoring the 80th anniversary of Pininfarina and all were sold before the car was unveiled to the public.

name: 599 GTB 60F1 2011
description: In December 2011, Ferrari announced a special edition of the 599 GTB to celebrate 60 years of their wins in F1. From Froilán González's win at Silverstone in 1951 with the 375 F1, to Fernando Alonso's win at Silverstone in 2011 in the 150° Italia, Ferrari have been winning hundreds of F1 races. The car was based on the 599 HGTE chassis, that utilised stiff suspension, shorter springs and stiffer anti-roll bars. The new 20" diamond-finished forged alloy wheels and Scuderia Ferrari shields came on the body as standard.

name: Superamerica 45 2011
description: The Ferrari Superamerica 45 is a one-off with a rotating targa hardtop based on the 599 GTB, that was commissioned to Ferrari by longtime Ferrari enthusiast and collector Peter Kalikow to commemorate his 45 years as a Ferrari client. Part of Ferrari's Special Project programme, it was designed at Centro Stile Ferrari and engineered in-house by Ferrari. The car made its public debut at Concorso d'Eleganza Villa d'Este on 20 May 2011. The carbon fibre hardtop roof incorporates a rear screen and rotates around a horizontal axis behind the cabin, a mechanism similar to that of the 2005 Ferrari Superamerica. The boot, also made from carbon fibre, has been redesigned to house the roof when open and to increase downforce at the rear of the car. As well as being finished in Blu Antille, other key visual features include a chromed front grille, body coloured wheels, carbon fibre panels and special burnished aluminum A-pillars, wing mirrors and door handles.

name: SP30 2013
description: The Ferrari SP30 is a one-off based on the 599 GTO and completed in 2013. It is the fifth project completed by Ferrari's special projects division and it includes elements from the 599XX and the F12 which was released a few months earlier. It was commissioned by Cheerag Arya, the owner of a petrochemical company. The car was then seized by the government of Abu Dhabi and was exported to the USA where it still resides today.

name: F12berlinetta 2013
description: The Ferrari F12berlinetta (Type F152) is a front engine, rear-wheel-drive grand tourer produced by Italian automobile manufacturer Ferrari. The F12berlinetta debuted at the 2012 Geneva Motor Show, and replaced the 599 grand tourer.

name: F60 America 2014
description: The Ferrari F60 America is a limited production roadster derivative of the F12, built to celebrate 60 years of Ferrari in North America. Production was limited to ten examples and according to the manufacturer, all were already spoken for at the time of the car's public introduction in October 2014. The open-top configuration pays homage to US-only limited production convertible Ferraris of the past, such as the 275 GTS/4 NART Spider. Compared to the F12, the F60 has all-new bodywork; it uses a light removable soft top instead of a conventional convertible top. Each of the ten examples built features an asymmetrical cabin design with red trim for the driver's area and black for the passenger, American flag detailing in the center of the backrests of both seats, and classic blue and white North American Racing Team livery.

name: SP America 2014
description: The Ferrari SP America is a one-off coupé based on the F12berlinetta completed in 2014 and developed under Ferrari's Special Projects programme with Pininfarina. It features custom bodywork, including a redesigned hood with three vents, which many reviewers say is reminiscent of the 250 GTO. It also has unique vents on the fenders and slats behind the windows, as well as a redesigned front bumper and redesigned rear end with a large diffuser and an integrated spoiler. The car was commissioned by Danny Wegman, CEO of Wegmans.

name: F12 TRS 2014
description: The Ferrari F12 TRS is a barchetta based on the F12berlinetta, made for a customer by Ferrari under its Special Projects programme. It made its public debut in Sicily at the Ferrari Cavalcade in June 2014. Designed in-house by the Centro Stile Ferrari headed by Flavio Manzoni, it took inspiration from the 1957 250 Testa Rossa, reinterpreted in a modern design language. The car has a Barchetta bodystyle, harking back to the original Testarossa. A redesigned rear section also recalls the styling of the Testarossa in a modern fashion. The front of the car features a redesigned front bumper and a new engine cover with a glass panel displaying the V12 engine's cylinder heads.

name: Touring Superleggera Aero 3 2015
description: The Touring Superleggera Aero 3 is a limited production coachbuilt car by Carrozzeria Touring based on the F12berlinetta. The design of the car is inspired by the Alfa Romeo 8C 2900B that raced at Le Mans in the 1930s.[37] Only 15 cars are to be built.

name: SP275 RW Competizione 2015
description: The Ferrari SP275 RW Competizione is a one-off model inspired by the 1964 Ferrari 275 GTB/C Speciale that won in the GT class at the 1965 24 Hours of Le Mans and placed 3rd overall. Its design is a collaboration between the Ferrari Styling Centre and Pininfarina. The car is based on the F12berlinetta but utilises the 6.3 liter V12 engine from the F12tdf which generates a power output of 780 PS (574 kW; 769 hp) and 705 N⋅m (520 lb⋅ft) of torque. The car has a coachbuilt body with a redesigned front fascia and headlights, widened rear track, custom 20 inch 10 spoke forged alloy wheels, an aluminium fuel filler cap and unique yellow exterior colour. The body also has 3 vents behind each side window, 3 on each side of the rear bumper and 4 vents on each fender inspired by the vents on the racing 275 GTB/C. The "RW" designation stands for the initials of the person who commissioned the car, Florida dentist Rick Workman, who also owns the 599-based Ferrari P540 Superfast Aperta unveiled in 2009 and the F12-based F60 America mentioned above that was unveiled in 2014.

name: F12berlinetta SG50 Edition 2015
description: The Ferrari F12berlinetta SG50 Edition is a one-off made to celebrate Singapore's 50 years of independence. Changes over the F12berlinetta include a red and white livery featuring a special ‘Rosso Singapore’ paintwork on the exterior and leather upholstery in the same colour on the interior. The colour scheme is designed to mirror the colors of the Singapore flag. Additionally the car has Singaporean lions embroidered in the headrest as well as the words "Singapore 50th Anniversary Edition 1/1" on the steering wheel and the door sills.

name: Touring Berlinetta Lusso 2015
description: At the March 2015 Geneva Motor Show Carrozzeria Touring unveiled the Touring Berlinetta Lusso, a two-seater, three-box coupé based on the F12berlinetta. Carrozzeria Touring developed the car on a client's request. The car has coachbuilt bodywork with each body panel of the car made from hand beaten aluminium. It also features a special blue exterior colour and a redesigned interior. The mechanical components are the same as the F12berlinetta, as are the headlamps and tail lamps. A production run of five examples was announced.

name: F12tdf 2016
description: The Ferrari F12tdf (tour de France) is a track-focused version of the F12berlinetta unveiled in October 2015. The name pays homage to the Tour de France automobile race held between 1899 and 1986 and which was regularly won by the Ferrari 250 between 1956 and 1964. The F12tdf shares the same 6.3 litre V12 engine with the F12berlinetta, but with power output increased to 769 hp. The F12tdf is 110 kg lighter than the F12. Ferrari claims an acceleration time for the F12tdf of 0 to 100 km/h in 2.9 seconds and 0 to 200 km/h in 7.9 seconds. The top speed was claimed to be 'in excess of 340 km/h'.

name: 812 Superfast 2018
description: The Ferrari 812 Superfast (Type F152M) is a front mid-engine, rear-wheel-drive grand tourer that made its debut at the 2017 Geneva Motor Show. The 812 Superfast is the successor to the F12berlinetta.

name: 812 Superfast Mole 2019
description: The Ferrari 812 Superfast (Type F152M) is a front mid-engine, rear-wheel-drive grand tourer that made its debut at the 2017 Geneva Motor Show. The 812 Superfast is the successor to the F12berlinetta.

name: Omologata 2020
description: The Ferrari Omologata was unveiled on 25 September 2020. It is a unique model produced for a wealthy European client of the manufacturer. The Omologata is the tenth “V12 engine in front position” project of the special “one-off” department since the 2009 Ferrari P 540 Superfast Aperta.

name: 812 GTS 2020
description: Unveiled in September 2019, the Ferrari 812 GTS is the open top version of the 812 Superfast. This marks the first front-engine V12 series production convertible model offered by Ferrari in 50 years, as the convertible variants of the 550, the 575 and the 599 were limited edition models meant for special customers only. The large rear buttresses present at the rear hold the folding hard top roof under a tonneau cover present between them when not in use. The electronically operated hard top takes 14 seconds for operation and is operable at speeds up to 45 km/h (28 mph). The GTS weighs 75 kg (165 lb) more than the Superfast due to chassis reinforcing components but maintains equal performance. The mechanical components including the engine remain the same as the Superfast except for the transmission which has shorter gear ratios to improve the car's response to throttle inputs. The engine's high-pressure injection system reduces the number of particles that are emitted before the catalytic converter warms up. There is also a new gasoline particulate filter and a stop-start system to improve fuel economy. Other features shared with the Superfast include the Manettino dial, side-slip angle control and variable steering weight. The car has been aerodynamically refined in order to eliminate any turbulence arising from the loss of a fixed roof.

name: SP51 2022
description: The Ferrari SP51 is a one-off based on the Ferrari 812 GTS developed upon a special request of a client in Taiwan. The car is a unique V12 roadster with total absence of a roof. The front end of the car is redesigned and features unique headlights which are shorter and much less angled than those on the 812 GTS. The bodywork is painted in ‘Rosso Passionale’ three-layer paintwork developed specifically for the car and the blue and white livery inspired by a legendary 1955 Ferrari 410 S

name: 812 Competizione Tailor Made 2023
description: At an exclusive event held at Casa Ferrari during the 2023 Monterey Car Week, the Maranello-based company unveiled a one-of-a-kind Ferrari 812 Competizione. Without precedent, this Tailor Made car was inspired by the concept of a blank sheet, which is how the Ferrari Styling Centre starts its creative research for every new model. The Tailor Made concept is applied to is one of just 999 Ferrari 812 Competiziones, a limited and extremely exclusive series intended for collectors and enthusiasts of the world's most exciting twelve-cylinder. It is distinguished by a commemorative plaque affixed to the interior as a testament to its original inspiration. This Tailor Made 812 Competizione was commissioned by Ferrari North America and will be auctioned at the Ferrari Gala held in New York City on October 17, 2023 at the culmination of an exclusive event aimed at celebrating the Maranello-based company's unique community and passion for the brand. All proceeds will be donated to charity as part of the education support projects that have been the focal point of the Prancing Horse's charitable activities.

name: 12Cilindri 2024
description: Since 1947, the heart-pounding thrill of Ferrari's naturally-aspirated, mid-front-mounted V12 engines has been synonymous with the brand, starting with the first engine roaring through the gates at Maranello. The latest model, the Ferrari 12Cilindri, was revealed in Miami Beach to mark 70 years of Ferrari in the American market. Drawing inspiration from the legendary Grand Tourers of the 1950s and '60s, this car combines elegance, versatility, and high performance. Its design is marked by sleek, harmonious lines, an active aerodynamic device, and iconic twin tailpipes. The 12Cilindri features an evolved V12 engine, now producing 830 horsepower, with a redline at 9500 rpm. The design enhancements aim to deliver smooth, seamless power, reaching peak power at maximum revs. This includes lighter components such as titanium connecting rods and aluminum alloy pistons, and a valvetrain optimized through Formula 1-derived technology to reduce friction and enhance performance. This model also introduces the Aspirated Torque Shaping (ATS) to enhance the driving experience by tailoring torque delivery in specific gears, optimizing acceleration and driving pleasure. The car's exhaust system complies with stringent emissions standards, incorporating advanced technology like a ceramic catalytic converter and a particulate filter. The Ferrari 12Cilindri's cockpit is designed for utmost comfort with a spacious feel, enhanced by a glass roof and high-quality materials. It supports a unique driving experience with an 8-speed Dual Clutch Transmission (DCT), praised for its efficiency and engaging performance across all gears. The integration of larger tires on 21" rims enhances the longitudinal performance and gear shifting times, making it a distinctive choice for driving enthusiasts who value Ferrari's rich heritage and cutting-edge technology.

name: 12Cilindri Spider 2024
description: The Ferrari 12Cilindri Spider, designed by Flavio Manzoni and the Ferrari Styling Centre team, represents a significant shift in styling compared to previous Ferrari models like the 812 Competizione A. Embracing a more refined design language, the car maintains Ferrari's iconic sensual appeal but with a greater emphasis on geometric precision and functional forms, moving away from nostalgic influences. The design features clean, interconnected lines and volumes, creating a sleek and seamless appearance. The muscularity of the rear wing is controlled, extending slightly into the front wing, enhancing the car's monolithic look. The bonnet merges into the front wings, creating a fluid, uninterrupted surface punctuated only by vents for engine cooling. The front of the Spider departs from traditional Ferrari elements, opting for a minimalist approach with integrated headlights forming a single wraparound band. Distinctive to the 12Cilindri Spider are the rear buttresses, which add a bold character to the design, and are complemented by a black-screen effect. This motif is also evident in the flank's 'quarterlight' made from back-painted polycarbonate. The rear maintains this strict design discipline, with taillights integrated into a blade that runs across the concave back, emphasizing the vehicle’s high-tech aesthetic. In place of a traditional rear spoiler, the design includes two active flaps integrated with the rear screen, contributing to the car's high-tech theme. The cabin is framed in body color, harmonizing with the overall design, and the tail section concludes in a clean, monolithic form, with the lower part featuring prominent diffuser keels, which alongside the twin tailpipes, enhances the perception of a floating car body. The design cleverly balances technical functionality with aesthetic appeal, presenting a fresh take on the Ferrari design ethos.

name: 365 P Pininfarina Tre Posti Speciale 1966
description: Enzo Ferrari was initially conservative about adapting mid-engine layouts for road cars, despite embracing the configuration for Ferrari’s Formula 1 and sports racing cars. Ferrari had quickly shifted to mid-mounted engines in racing, becoming the first team after Cooper to win the F1 World Championship with a mid-engine car and achieving the first outright Le Mans win with this setup. However, Enzo resisted the idea for road cars, concerned that the handling of a mid-engine V12 would be too challenging for anyone but professional drivers. Sergio Pininfarina, Ferrari's longtime collaborator and the designer behind many of its iconic road cars, saw untapped potential in creating a mid-engine Ferrari for the public. Pininfarina believed that such a model would broaden Ferrari's appeal and allow for more radical design expression. To convince Enzo, he introduced various mid-engine show cars, including the road-going 250 LM prototype displayed at the 1965 Geneva Motor Show. Though Enzo remained skeptical, prominent Ferrari clients like Luigi Chinetti and Gianni Agnelli were enthusiastic, motivating Pininfarina to push forward with a mid-engined Ferrari concept. For the 1966 show season, Pininfarina crafted a new mid-engine model using underpinnings from Ferrari’s 330 P2 sports cars, which had raced at Le Mans. This platform was modified with a slightly larger single-cam 4.4-liter V12 engine—known as the 365 P specification—producing around 380 horsepower. The V12 was paired with a five-speed transaxle and installed in a chassis that combined a multi-tubular space frame with aluminum panels for extra rigidity. This semi-monocoque structure mirrored Ferrari’s F1 designs of the era, offering both strength and lightness. The 365 P’s chassis featured independent double-wishbone suspension with coil-over shocks on all four corners, ensuring advanced handling characteristics suitable for a high-performance sports car. This configuration, along with the powerful V12, laid the groundwork for Ferrari’s later road-going mid-engine models. Although Enzo was slow to approve mid-engine road cars, the Pininfarina concept ultimately paved the way for a new era of Ferrari design and performance, leading to the eventual production of iconic models like the 206 and 246 Dinos and, later, the Berlinetta Boxer series.

name: 365 California Spyder 1966–1967
description: The Ferrari 365 California debuted in 1966 as a successor to the 500 Superfast, marking the start of Ferrari's 365 series. It featured a 4.4-liter Colombo V12 engine, an upgrade from the 330’s 4.0-liter unit, with an 81 mm bore that helped generate 320 horsepower. This engine setup, which included three Weber carburetors, provided ample power for a high-performance, grand-touring experience. The 365 California shared its chassis with the 500 Superfast but showcased a refined cabriolet body crafted by Pininfarina. It was introduced at the Geneva Motor Show in 1966, where it drew attention for its sleek, sophisticated design. Only 14 units were produced, making it one of Ferrari's rarest models, with just two examples built in right-hand drive. The car’s rarity and exclusivity are part of what makes it so desirable among collectors today. Initially, the prototype of the 365 California was based on the 330 GT 2+2’s type 571 chassis, but the production models were built on the updated type 598 chassis. These chassis were sent to Pininfarina’s facility in Grugliasco for the bodywork and interior trimming, before being returned to Ferrari for the final assembly of the mechanical components. This collaborative production process contributed to the model's distinct character and high-quality craftsmanship. Production wrapped up in 1967, cementing the 365 California as a short-lived yet iconic chapter in Ferrari’s history.

name: 365 GTC 1968 - 1969
description: The Ferrari 330 GTC was introduced at the 1966 Geneva Motor Show as a grand tourer positioned between the sporty 275 GTB and the more practical 330 GT 2+2. Blending elements from both, it used the 275 GTB’s advanced all-independent suspension chassis and the 330 GT 2+2’s four-liter Colombo V12 engine. With styling cues borrowed from the 275 GTS and the headlights from the 500 Superfast, the 330 GTC emerged as an elegant and balanced design that upheld Ferrari's reputation for producing beautiful, high-performance grand tourers. A key feature of the 330 GTC was its fully independent rear suspension, a departure from the live axle design that had been common on Ferrari's previous grand tourers. The car’s transaxle, combining the rear suspension with the gearbox, helped achieve a balanced weight distribution on its 2400 mm short wheelbase, making the 330 GTC nimble and responsive. The 4.0-liter V12, equipped with three Weber carburetors, delivered 300 horsepower, providing ample power for a car of its class. Paired with a five-speed gearbox, the 330 GTC offered smooth and flexible performance suited for long-distance driving. The car’s bodywork was crafted by Pininfarina, combining elements from earlier models into a cohesive and attractive design. Although it was not an entirely original look, the mix of the 275 GTS’s flowing lines and the 500 Superfast’s headlight style resulted in a car that many regard as one of the most visually appealing Ferraris of its era. Production of the 330 GTC continued until 1968, when it was replaced by the 365 GTC, which featured a larger 4.4-liter engine and slight design tweaks, most notably the removal of vents behind the front wheels. The 365 GTC’s production was short-lived due to evolving safety and emissions regulations in the United States, which affected many high-performance European cars at the time. In 1971, Ferrari revived the GTC name briefly with the 365 GTC/4, derived from the Daytona, but this model too had a limited production run of about 500 units before it was discontinued. This marked the end of Ferrari’s classic GTC line, which had only spanned a few years but left a lasting impression. The 330 and 365 GTC models remain highly valued among collectors, appreciated for their combination of performance, handling, and timeless design. A notable example of a 365 GTC sold at Bonham's Ferrari Sale in 2005, achieving a high price that reflected its desirability and the enduring appeal of this elegant series.

name: 365 GTS 1968 - 1969
description: Ferrari’s first production convertible, the 250 GT Cabriolet, was introduced in 1957, establishing a new segment in the brand’s lineup. After the discontinuation of the Series II 250 GT Cabriolet in 1962, Ferrari took a brief hiatus from producing convertibles, leaving customers waiting for a new open-top model. When Ferrari finally introduced the replacement in 1964, it came in the form of the 275 GTS, based on the stunning 275 GTB coupe that had debuted the same year. While the 275 GTB featured a more aggressive, enclosed headlight design, the 275 GTS took styling cues from the earlier 250 GT Cabriolet, giving it a more classic, open-front look. The 275 GTS marked a significant technical leap forward for Ferrari. It was the first road-going model to feature all-independent suspension, replacing the live rear axle that had been standard on the 250 series. Under the hood, the 275 GTS was powered by a 3.3-liter SOHC V12 engine, delivering 260 horsepower—slightly less than the GTB’s more powerful setup but optimized for smoother, easier driving at lower speeds. The car also introduced a rear-mounted five-speed transaxle, improving weight distribution and handling. The styling of the 275 GTS, designed by Pininfarina, set it apart from its Berlinetta counterpart, but it wasn’t universally loved. Its design was more understated compared to the sharper, sportier lines of the 275 GTB, which led to mixed opinions among Ferrari enthusiasts. Despite this, the 275 GTS found a respectable audience, with 200 units produced between 1964 and 1966—a significant figure for Ferrari convertibles of the era. In 1966, the 275 GTS was succeeded by the 330 GTS, which featured a slightly larger engine and a more pronounced front-end design. This line of Ferrari convertibles culminated with the very limited 365 GTS in 1969, with only 20 units produced. Following this model, Ferrari would not produce a high-volume convertible until the introduction of the Ferrari California in the late 2000s, making the 275 GTS and its successors rare and highly prized among collectors.

name: 365 GTB/4 "Daytona" 1968 - 1974
description: The Ferrari 365 GTB/4, commonly known as the "Daytona," made a significant impact when it debuted at the 1968 Paris Motor Show. Though the car was expected to officially bear the "Daytona" name in celebration of Ferrari's 1-2-3 finish at the 1967 Daytona 24 Hours, Ferrari ultimately stuck to their conventional naming system, labeling it the 365 GTB/4. Despite this, the nickname "Daytona" caught on and remains widely used to this day. In a competitive landscape dominated by the mid-engined Lamborghini Miura and DeTomaso Pantera, Ferrari's decision to keep the Daytona front-engined was notable. While Ferrari had plenty of experience building mid-engined race cars, they chose to stay with the familiar front-engine, rear-wheel-drive setup for their flagship GT. The Daytona’s 4.4-liter V12 was based on the 330’s engine, featuring dual overhead camshafts, a recent addition to Ferrari's production models. The car’s structure followed the traditional oval tube ladder frame design, reinforcing its classic Ferrari lineage. The Daytona’s styling by Pininfarina marked a sharp departure from Ferrari’s previous GT designs. The car's angular, aggressive lines, particularly at the front, gave it a more modern, distinctive look compared to the softer, rounded forms of earlier Ferraris. Early models featured a full-width plastic headlight cover, but due to U.S. regulations, this was replaced with pop-up headlights starting in 1971. The new design enhanced the car’s appearance, and pop-up headlights became standard on all Daytonas. The Daytona was produced until 1974, with nearly 1,400 units built. This model marked the end of an era for Ferrari’s front-engine GT berlinettas, as it was succeeded by the mid-engined 365 GT4/BB. Ferrari wouldn’t return to the front-engine GT layout until the launch of the 550 Maranello in the late 1990s, making the Daytona a last classic in its line. Today, the 365 GTB/4 is celebrated not only for its performance but also for its iconic design, which set it apart in a period of intense innovation in the automotive world.

name: 365 GTS/4 "Daytona" 1969 - 1973
description: The Ferrari 365 GTB/4, famously known as the "Daytona," made its debut at the 1968 Paris Motor Show, quickly becoming a high-profile release. While the press expected it to be officially named after Ferrari's 1-2-3 finish at the 1967 Daytona 24 Hours, Ferrari ultimately labeled it the 365 GTB/4 in line with their traditional naming conventions. Despite this, the nickname "Daytona" stuck and is still widely used today. In the late 1960s, Ferrari faced fierce competition from mid-engined rivals like the Lamborghini Miura and DeTomaso Pantera. However, Enzo Ferrari chose to stay with a front-engine, rear-wheel-drive layout, emphasizing his preference for the classic configuration. The Daytona’s V12 engine, derived from the 330 model, featured double overhead camshafts—an advanced feature that had only recently been introduced in Ferrari’s production cars. Its chassis used a traditional oval tube ladder frame, grounding it firmly in Ferrari’s established engineering approach. Designed by Pininfarina, the Daytona stood out with its sharper, more angular lines, especially in the front, which departed from the softer designs of earlier Ferrari GTs. Early models had a unique full-width plastic strip at the front to cover the headlights, but this feature had to be changed for the U.S. market due to regulatory issues. In 1971, retractable headlights became standard, enhancing the car's sleek appearance even further. A year after the launch of the Berlinetta, Ferrari introduced an open-top version, the Daytona GTS. Produced in very limited numbers, the GTS is now much rarer than the coupe, with only a small fraction built compared to the Berlinetta. This rarity has driven up the GTS’s value significantly, making it worth roughly three times as much as the GTB today. Many GTB owners have even converted their Berlinettas to GTS specifications to emulate the desirable open-top model.

name: 365 GTC/4 1971 - 1972
description: The Ferrari 365 GTC/4 was launched in 1971 as a refined, more luxurious alternative to the 365 GTB/4 Daytona. Though it shared the Daytona's general design and chassis, the GTC/4 was lengthened slightly to allow for a small rear seat, which was primarily suitable for children. This model emphasized comfort and practicality, with features like power steering and power-assisted brakes, making it a more relaxed grand tourer ideal for long-distance driving. Under the hood, the 365 GTC/4 retained the Daytona’s impressive four-cam, 4.4-liter V12 engine with a unitary displacement of 365cc per cylinder. However, the GTC/4’s version was slightly detuned, producing 320 horsepower—about 30 horsepower less than the Daytona’s more performance-focused setup. Unlike the transaxle layout in the Daytona, the GTC/4’s gearbox was mounted directly to the engine, which slightly altered its weight distribution and handling characteristics. The design of the 365 GTC/4 was a softened interpretation of the Daytona’s bold, angular look. Pininfarina gave the car smoother, more understated lines while retaining key elements like the pop-up headlights and a distinctive grille surround in a contrasting color. The body was constructed from steel, with aluminum used for the doors, engine cover, and rear deck lid, combining durability with lighter materials where possible. Production of the 365 GTC/4 lasted only about 18 months, with just 500 units built, making it one of Ferrari’s rarer grand tourers. It wasn’t directly replaced but was eventually succeeded by the 365 GT4 2+2 in 1972, which continued Ferrari’s emphasis on more luxurious, spacious models. While the GTC/4 remains less iconic than the Daytona, it has gradually gained appreciation for its blend of power, comfort, and exclusivity, offering a more affordable entry into Ferrari's classic V12 grand touring lineup.

name: Ferrari 365 GTB/4 Daytona Group 4 1971 - 1976
description: Throughout the early 1960s, Ferrari was known for its successful GT racers, but by the late 1960s, the company shifted its focus more exclusively to Formula 1 and sports prototypes, stepping back from producing GT cars specifically for racing. This led to tensions with racing authorities over homologation issues, making it difficult to turn Ferrari’s newer road cars into competitive GT racers. As a result, Ferrari officially wound down its customer GT racing program by the end of 1966, though many of Ferrari’s loyal distributors and prominent clients remained eager to compete. Chief among them was Luigi Chinetti and his North American Racing Team (NART), who had no intention of giving up on racing Ferraris in the GT category. When Ferrari introduced the 365 GTB/4 “Daytona” in 1968, Chinetti quickly seized the opportunity to adapt it for endurance racing, fielding a modified version in the 1969 24 Hours of Le Mans. The car he used was a lightweight, aluminum-bodied Daytona prepared in Italy with tacit support from Ferrari. Modifications focused on weight reduction, suspension upgrades, and fitting wider tires to improve grip. The Daytona's twin-cam V12 engine, the first of its kind in a Ferrari road car, was also tuned for racing. However, the Daytona’s debut at Le Mans was rough; a crash in practice meant it couldn’t even start the race. Subsequent races at Sebring and Daytona also proved challenging, but NART’s efforts sparked interest among other privateers, and soon more Daytonas were prepared for competition. By 1971, Ferrari officially sanctioned a “Competizione” version of the Daytona, developed by Michelotto and produced in three series of five cars each. These Competizione Daytonas featured further refinements for Group 4 racing, and Michelotto also supplied components to privateers like NART who were building their own versions. The Daytona Competizione models enjoyed considerable success in the 1970s, achieving outright wins in the Tour de France and multiple class victories at endurance events like Le Mans. Among the more unique adaptations was a special Daytona Spyder built by NART for the 1975 Le Mans—a rare example of a convertible adapted for racing. When the mid-engined 365 GT4 Berlinetta Boxer (BB) replaced the Daytona in 1974, Ferrari aimed to continue its GT racing tradition with the new model, but the BB proved much harder to make competitive. Its rear-heavy weight distribution and aerodynamic challenges required substantial factory assistance, and Pininfarina eventually designed a new body to improve its performance on the track. Despite these efforts, the BB never achieved the same level of success as the Daytona in GT racing. In later years, Ferrari acknowledged the significance of these privateer-built Daytona Competizione models by allowing some of them to compete in the now-defunct Ferrari-Maserati Challenge. Although not all period-modified Daytonas were included in this series, many of the original competition cars remain highly prized. Since the 1970s, additional Daytonas have been converted for track use or spirited driving, further cementing the Daytona’s legacy as one of Ferrari’s most enduring and versatile GT models.

name: 365 GTB/4 Daytona Competizione S2 1972
description: When Ferrari launched the 365 GTB/4 "Daytona" in 1968 without an official competition version, it marked a shift from the company’s long tradition of dominating GT racing with models like the 250 GT. Rising costs in Formula 1 and prototype racing, along with Ferrari’s shift in focus away from customer GT racing, had led to the end of Ferrari’s official GT competition program. However, the Daytona still retained the racing DNA of its predecessors, and it wasn’t long before Luigi Chinetti, Ferrari’s American importer and head of the North American Racing Team (NART), prepared a Daytona for the track. Chinetti's first modified Daytona debuted in 1969, competing at major endurance races such as the 24 Hours of Daytona and the 12 Hours of Sebring. Impressed with the potential of his race-prepped Daytona, Chinetti approached Enzo Ferrari to reconsider producing a dedicated competition version. Ferrari agreed to a compromise: the customer assistance department, "Assistenza Clienti," would prepare a small batch of Daytonas to racing specifications, but Ferrari’s official racing department, "Gestione Sportiva," would remain uninvolved. This allowed Ferrari to maintain some distance from the racing program while still supporting loyal privateer clients. Built to meet Group 4 regulations, the "Daytona Competizione" featured extensive modifications. The car’s body was made entirely from aluminum with plastic windows, reducing the car's weight by 400 kg. Bumpers were removed, and small aerodynamic "fences" were added on the front fenders to improve stability at high speeds. Engine modifications included a cold air intake and open exhaust, which boosted output to 402 horsepower. Five of these lightweight Daytonas were built for the 1971 season, and they quickly found success. In their debut at the Tour de France, two of these cars finished fourth and tenth overall, while a car entered in the Montlhéry 1000 km placed third overall. For 1972, Ferrari’s Assistenza Clienti built an additional five cars with steel bodies and a further refined engine producing 430 horsepower. These "Series 2" Daytonas achieved major victories, with a notable class win at the 24 Hours of Le Mans where Charles Pozzi’s Daytona finished fifth overall and first in class. The same car also won the Tour de France the following year, highlighting the Daytona Competizione’s versatility. Across the Atlantic, Daytonas continued to be competitive, proving their strength in American endurance racing as well. In 1973, Ferrari produced a final batch of five competition Daytonas with even more powerful engines for the new season. These final examples remained competitive, taking additional class wins at Le Mans. In total, Ferrari’s Assistenza Clienti produced 15 competition Daytonas, with several more road-going Daytonas later converted to Group 4 specifications by privateers. One of these converted Daytonas even managed to finish second overall at the Daytona 24 Hours in 1979, underscoring the model’s enduring competitiveness. With three class victories at Le Mans and an outright win in the Tour de France, the Daytona Competizione earned a lasting place in Ferrari’s legacy of successful GT cars. Though developed outside Ferrari’s main racing department, these client-prepared cars achieved remarkable results. Today, the Daytona Competizione is highly valued by collectors and is eligible for prestigious historic events like the Tour Auto and Le Mans Classic, where its racing heritage is celebrated.

name: 456 GT 1992-1997
description: The 456 GT took the luxury 2+2 coupé theme to new heights and saw Ferrari return to the front-engine concept for the first time since the 1985 412. The all-new 65° V12 provided unprecedented flexibility and power.

name: 456 GTA 1992-1997
description: This was the automatic version of the 456 GT. Supplied as an option on the 400 Automatic from 1976, the automatic transmission was very popular and much in demand on the North American and Pacific markets. The 4-speed gearbox’s electronic management system adapted automatically to different driving styles, dispensing with the need for a kick-down mode.

name: 456M GT 1998-2003
description: The 456M, in its GT and automatic gearbox GTA guises, is the evolution of Ferrari’s highly successful interpretation of its traditional 2+2 models. First introduced in 1992, the 456 GT was the marque’s first car designed with the 21st century in mind and has been much appreciated by Maranello’s clients for the way it provides saloon-class comfort and roominess combined with class-leading performance.

name: 456M GTA 1998-2003
description: The 456M, in its GT and automatic gearbox GTA guises, is the evolution of Ferrari’s highly successful interpretation of its traditional 2+2 models. First introduced in 1992, the 456 GT was the marque’s first car designed with the 21st century in mind and has been much appreciated by Maranello’s clients for the way it provides saloon-class comfort and roominess combined with class-leading performance. A true four-seater coupé, the 456 GT was also the first modern Ferrari to revert to a front engine, rear-wheel drive layout, with the gearbox in unit with the rear differential for ideal weight distribution as well as plenty of space for passengers and luggage.

name: 612 Scaglietti 2004
description: The Ferrari 612 Scaglietti (Type F137) is an executive 2+2 coupé grand tourer manufactured between 2004 and 2010. It was designed to replace the smaller 456; its larger size makes it a true 4 seater with adequate space in the rear seats for adults. The design, especially the large side scallops and the headlights, pays homage to the 375 MM built in 1954 that director Roberto Rossellini had commissioned for his wife, Ingrid Bergman.

name: GG50 2005
description: The Ferrari GG50 is a concept car developed by Italian styling house Italdesign Giugiaro, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Giorgetto Giugiaro as an automobile designer. Based on the 612 Scaglietti, the car was designed by Giugiaro himself and has a fastback coupé body style with a shorter wheelbase than the donor car. The car features folding back seats and a tailgate in order for easy entry and exit of the passengers. The folding back seats also result in increased storage space. The roofline has been designed in a downsloping manner, resulting in more headroom for passengers in the rear seats. Apart from that, the car features a redesigned nose, unique wheels, a glass roof, front air vents near the front wheels for increased downforce and improved brake cooling, thinner headlamps and unique quad tail lamps. Giugiaro wanted a Ferrari that he could easily use with his family and which allowed easy entry and exit of passengers from the rear of the car. The mechanical components are the same as those found in a standard 612. The car was unveiled at the 2005 Tokyo Motor Show. Ferrari provided a rolling chassis of the 612 Scaglietti and technical support to Italdesign during the build process.

name: 612 Kappa 2006
description: The 612 Kappa is a one-off special built by Pininfarina for Peter S. Kalikow, former chairman of the New York Metropolitan Transport Authority, and a renowned Ferrari collector, and unveiled at the Villa d'Este Concours d'Elegance on April 23, 2006. The Kappa stands out from the standard 612 through the use of multiple unique elements, such as a functional hood scoop on the engine cover lined with polished aluminium, added air vents near the front wheels for improved downforce, the rear taillights found on the Ferrari Enzo along with the F430, personalised door handles containing the initials of the owner, chromed headlight bezels, and a sunroof that utilises electrochromic glass to vary the amount of opacity in the glass. On the request of Kalikow, Ferrari agreed to not copy the distinguished elements of the 612 Kappa into its future models.

name: 612 GP Berne Edition 2006
description: Unveiled at Montreux Grand Prix Switzerland on July 22, 2006, the 612 GP program was in celebration of Ferrari's 40th anniversary in Switzerland and the anniversary of their first Swiss Grand Prix in 1949. The dark grey car - of which 9 were built - featured the HGTC handling package (including carbon-ceramic brakes) bi-colour paint treatment with the 612 Scaglietti's side "scallops" finished in a silver-grey color and color-coded brake calipers. The interior was finished in red with grey trim. The lower dashboard, seat front sections, central tunnel sides, door panels, and lower half of the sports steering wheel all finished in red. A special plaque, imprinted with an outline of the historic Berne track, is mounted on the dash.

name: Cornes 30th Anniversary Edition 2006
description: Unveiled June 2006 in Japan, the Cornes 30th Anniversary Edition is a limited (20 unit) variant for the Japanese market commemorating the 30th anniversary of Cornes & Co. importing Ferrari automobiles into Japan. Standard equipment included the HGTC package, Blu Cornes paint scheme, a carbon fibre filler cap, and mesh grille inserts in the front and rear fascias.

name: 612 Pebble Beach Bicolore 2006
description: One-off two-tone 612 Scaglietti special produced which would go on to influence one of the two colors of Sessanta models offered the following year (along with its sister car the 2006 Detroit Motor Show car). With its distinctive Nero and Silver paint scheme, the 612 Pebble Beach was displayed August 2005 at a private event during Pebble Beach Concours d'Elegance. Press articles in 2006 referred to this as the "Ferrari 612 Pebble Beach".

name: 612 Sessanta 2007
description: The 612 Sessanta (Italian for "sixty") is a limited (60 unit) variant of the 612 commemorating the 60th anniversary of the company. Standard equipment included 19-inch forged aluminium wheels, black chrome exhaust tips, the automated manual F1 transmission, three-position electrochromic glass roof, start/stop ignition button on the steering wheel, Bose infotainment system and a two-tone paint.

name: FF 2012
description: The Ferrari FF (Type F151) (FF meaning "Ferrari Four", for four seats and four-wheel drive) is a grand tourer presented on March 1, 2011, at the Geneva Motor Show as a successor to the 612 Scaglietti. It is Ferrari's first production four-wheel drive model. The body style has been described as a shooting brake, a type of two-door coupé-based sporting estate. The FF has a top speed of 335 km/h and it accelerates from 0 to 100 km/h in 3.7 seconds. Ferrari states that the FF was the world's fastest four-seat automobile upon its release to the public.

name: SP FFX 2014
description: The Ferrari FF (Type F151) (FF meaning "Ferrari Four", for four seats and four-wheel drive) is a grand tourer presented on March 1, 2011, at the Geneva Motor Show as a successor to the 612 Scaglietti. It is Ferrari's first production four-wheel drive model. The body style has been described as a shooting brake, a type of two-door coupé-based sporting estate. The FF has a top speed of 335 km/h and it accelerates from 0 to 100 km/h in 3.7 seconds. Ferrari states that the FF was the world's fastest four-seat automobile upon its release to the public.

name: GTC4 Lusso 2016-2020
description: Like its predecessor, the GTC4Lusso is a 3-door shooting brake with an all-wheel drive drivetrain, and is powered by a front-mid mounted V12 engine. The GTC4Lusso's 6,262 cc Ferrari F140 65° V12 engine is rated at 681 hp. Ferrari claims a top speed of 335 km/h, unchanged from the FF, and a 0–100 km/h acceleration time of 3.4 seconds. The car uses an improved version (called the 4RM Evo) of Ferrari's patented four-wheel drive system introduced on the FF, integrated with four-wheel steering into the system. Collectively, the system is called 4RM-S. The GTC4Lusso was unveiled at the 2016 Geneva Motor Show.

name: GTC4 Lusso T 2017-2020
description: Unveiled at the 2016 Paris Motor Show, the GTC4Lusso T is a rear wheel drive only version of the GTC4Lusso powered by a V8 engine with lesser displacement, though the 4WS four-wheel steering system from its V12 variant is retained.[8] The GTC4Lusso T comes with a 3,855 cc Ferrari F154 twin turbocharged V8 engine rated at 602 hp. According to the manufacturer the car can attain a top speed of over 320 km/h and accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in 3.5 seconds. On August 31, 2020, Ferrari confirmed the end of production for the GTC4Lusso. No replacement has been announced.

name: BR20 2021
description: The Ferrari BR20 is a one-off model created by Ferrari's Special Projects Programme and based on the V12-engined GTC4Lusso. It features redesigned bodywork that Ferrari says takes inspiration from past V12 Ferrari models such as the 410 Superamerica and 500 Superfast, with the biggest change being a new rear roofline that turns the car from a shooting brake into a fastback coupé. The new bodywork increases the overall length by three inches over the standard GTC4Lusso, and the rear seats were removed to accommodate the new sloping roofline. The interior features upholstery in two shades of brown leather, with carbon fiber and oak trim. Mechanically the car remains unchanged from the standard V12 GTC4Lusso. The price of this car has been not revealed and was developed for a long-standing customer. It currently resides in Saudi Arabia.

name: Purosangue 2023
description: Ferrari has introduced the Purosangue, its first-ever four-door, four-seater car, marking a significant milestone in the brand's 75-year history. Unveiled at the Teatro del Silenzio in Lajatico, Pisa, the Purosangue, which means 'thoroughbred' in Italian, embodies Ferrari's iconic DNA, combining performance, driving pleasure, and comfort in a unique way. The Purosangue features a distinct layout with a mid-front-mounted engine and a rear gearbox in a sporty transaxle arrangement, achieving an optimal 49:51% weight distribution. Unlike typical modern GTs with forward-mounted engines, this design enhances the car's driving dynamics significantly.
