It is the only car in its class to sport a naturally-aspirated V12 engine located mid-front, delivering the highest power in its segment at 725 horsepower and producing an enthralling Ferrari engine sound. The engine's design allows for 80% torque availability even at low revs, enhancing drivability. Aerodynamically, the Purosangue benefits from a synergy between the front bumper and wheelarch trim, creating an air curtain that improves airflow around the front wheels, reducing turbulence. The vehicle also incorporates Ferrari's latest dynamic control systems, such as independent four-wheel steering and a new active suspension system that minimizes body roll and optimizes tire contact, ensuring sports car-like performance and handling. The chassis includes a standard carbon-fibre roof to reduce weight and lower the center of gravity. The Purosangue's design also features rear-hinged back doors for easier access without compromising the vehicle's compactness. The interior offers four heated electric seats, ample cabin space for adults, and the largest boot ever in a Ferrari, with foldable rear seats to extend luggage capacity. Performance-wise, the Purosangue can accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in 3.3 seconds and from 0 to 200 km/h in 10.6 seconds. Despite its more commanding driving position, it maintains a low, intimate seating arrangement typical of Ferraris, connecting the driver closely to its dynamic capabilities. Standard luxury features include a Burmester© audio system, with optional extras like new Alcantara® upholstery made from certified recycled polyester, making the Purosangue a standout in its class for both performance and comfort.

name: Purosangue Handling Speciale 2027
description: The Ferrari Purosangue Handling Speciale is an optional configuration developed to sharpen the sporting character of Ferrari’s four-door, four-seater model. Designed for owners who want a more dynamic driving experience while keeping the car’s everyday versatility intact, it builds on the standard Purosangue’s transaxle layout with its front-mid mounted naturally-aspirated V12 engine and rear-mounted gearbox. The package focuses on enhancing responsiveness and driver engagement without altering the vehicle’s core four-seat layout or elevated driving position. Key mechanical and electronic revisions include a recalibrated active suspension system that reduces body movements by around 10 percent for a tighter, more composed feel through corners and direction changes. Gearshift strategies have been quickened, particularly in Race and ESC-Off modes, with more decisive changes during acceleration and at higher engine speeds in manual mode. The exhaust note is also more prominent at start-up and under load, further emphasizing the character of the 6.5-litre V12. Visually, the Handling Speciale stands out with several exclusive details that signal its sportier intent. These include a dedicated wheel design with diamond-cut finish, carbon-fibre side shields, matt black exhaust tips, a black rear Prancing Horse emblem, and a satin-finished Ferrari script. A special interior plaque identifies the configuration. Overall, the Purosangue Handling Speciale strengthens the model’s position as a true Ferrari by tilting its balance further toward performance while preserving its practical four-seater usability. It offers an appealing middle ground for enthusiasts seeking sharper dynamics in a comfortable, high-riding package suitable for daily driving.

name: Luce 2027
description: The Ferrari Luce is Ferrari’s first fully electric production car, unveiled in Rome as a landmark in the brand’s multi-energy future. Developed as part of a technologically neutral strategy, it represents a clean-sheet design rather than a simple electrification of an existing model. Created in collaboration with the design collective LoveFrom led by Sir Jony Ive and Marc Newson, the Luce introduces a new architectural approach with four electric motors (one per wheel), a 122 kWh battery, and a spacious four-door, five-seat layout — a first for Ferrari. It aims to blend exceptional performance with comfort, versatility, and a distinctive character while maintaining the brand’s core values of driving engagement. The exterior design is defined by remarkable visual purity, featuring an expansive, unbroken glass house that extends below the beltline, with floating front and rear aerodynamic wings creating a clean, shell-like silhouette. The car rides on the largest staggered wheels yet on a series Ferrari — 23-inch front and 24-inch rear — and incorporates active aerodynamic elements, including adjustable grilles and ride height. Launch colours include striking options such as Giallo Luce and Rosso Tramonto-inspired shades. The overall form prioritises smooth, continuous surfaces for both aesthetic simplicity and aerodynamic efficiency, resulting in the lowest drag coefficient in Ferrari’s road car history. Inside, the Luce offers a minimalist, airy cabin with a strong emphasis on intuitive driver interaction and material quality. The dual-cockpit layout features a unique steering assembly with physical controls, an innovative binnacle display combining analogue and digital elements, and multiple high-resolution OLED screens developed with Samsung. An advanced audio system with 21 speakers delivers a custom Ferrari Audio Signature. Special attention has been paid to comfort, with features like an integrated torque-control paddle system, a sophisticated key with E Ink display, and high-quality recycled and premium materials throughout. The five-seat configuration and generous interior space make it notably practical for a high-performance Ferrari. Technically, the Luce is powered by four in-house permanent magnet motors delivering a total of 1050 cv, with advanced torque vectoring and a proprietary Torque Shift Engagement system that gives the driver greater control over acceleration and regenerative braking. It achieves 0-100 km/h in 2.5 seconds and a top speed over 310 km/h, while offering more than 530 km of range. The car benefits from a highly integrated chassis-battery structure, active suspension, independent rear steering, and sophisticated electronic controls including a new Vehicle Control Unit. An authentic, mechanically-derived sound system enhances the driving experience without relying on artificial synthesis. Overall, the Luce positions itself as a new kind of Ferrari that expands the brand’s performance ethos into the electric era.

name: F355 Berlinetta 1994-1999
description: Ferrari significantly enhanced the 348 to create the F355, marking the introduction of a new generation in terms of performance and quality. Launched in 1994, the F355 initially came as a fixed head Berlinetta and later expanded to include Targa and Spyder models. By the time it was replaced around 1999/2000, Ferrari had produced over 11,000 units, making the F355 one of the most popular models in the brand's history and setting high performance benchmarks for its competitors. The F355 featured a 3.5-liter V8 engine that, although only stroked by 2 mm from the 348's engine, incorporated advanced technology, significantly boosting its output to 380 horsepower. This engine benefited from five valves per cylinder—three for intake and two for exhaust—operated by dual overhead camshafts with variable valve timing, a feature inspired by Ferrari’s Formula 1 technology. In 1998, the F355 lineup was further updated with a paddle-operated sequential gearbox, introduced in models designated as 'F1', enhancing the driving experience with F1-derived technology. The car's design, refined by Pininfarina, smoothed out the more aggressive lines of the 348 and removed the horizontal louvres from the side intakes, resulting in a cleaner aesthetic. Underneath its steel and aluminum body, the chassis and suspension systems were updated to significantly improve handling—addressing a common critique of the 348. The suspension was adjustable, offering settings for normal and sporty driving. Completing the performance enhancements, five-spoke alloy wheels concealed large ventilated Brembo discs, ensuring that the F355's stopping power matched its enhanced speed.

name: F355 Spider 1995-1999
description: Ferrari significantly enhanced the design of the 348 to create the F355, marking a new generation in terms of performance and quality. Launched in 1994 as a fixed head Berlinetta, the F355 lineup later included a Targa model and a full Spyder. By the time it was replaced in 1999/2000, over 11,000 units had been produced, making the F355 one of Ferrari's most popular models and setting high performance benchmarks for its competitors. The F355 featured a 3.5-liter V8 engine that, despite only being stroked by 2 mm from the 348's engine, incorporated advanced technology that boosted its output to 380 horsepower. This engine benefited from five valves per cylinder—three intake and two exhaust—operated by dual overhead camshafts with variable valve timing, a feature inspired by Ferrari’s Formula 1 technology. In 1998, the lineup was further updated with a paddle-operated sequential gearbox in the models designated as 'F1', enhancing the driving experience with F1-derived technology. The car's aesthetics, refined by Pininfarina, smoothed out the more aggressive lines of the 348 and eliminated the horizontal louvres from the side intakes, resulting in a cleaner look. Underneath its steel and aluminum body, the chassis and suspension systems were updated to significantly improve handling, a noted weakness of the 348. The suspension was adjustable, offering settings for normal and sporty driving. Completing the performance enhancements, five-spoke alloy wheels concealed large ventilated Brembo discs, ensuring that the F355's stopping power matched its enhanced speed.

name: F355 GTS 1995-1999
description: The Ferrari F355 GTS, introduced in 1995, is a variant of the F355 Berlinetta featuring a removable "targa-style" roof. It inherits the same mechanical and performance characteristics as the Berlinetta, including the 3.5-liter, 40-valve V8 engine that delivers 375 horsepower and 268 pound-feet of torque. This engine is paired with a six-speed manual gearbox, although an F1-style paddle-operated transmission was also available. This model was capable of reaching 0-62 mph in 4.7 seconds with a top speed of 183 mph. A notable aspect of the F355 GTS is its engine, which revs up to 8,250 rpm, producing a distinct, robust sound typical of Ferrari engines of that era. The F355 GTS shares many design elements with the 348 but features a more refined body that underwent extensive wind tunnel testing to improve aerodynamics. It was equipped with driver-adjustable suspension offering settings for different driving conditions and rode on five-spoke alloy wheels, behind which sat large ventilated Brembo disc brakes for effective stopping power. A total of 2,577 F355 GTS models were produced, with 2,048 featuring the manual gearbox and 529 equipped with the F1 transmission, making it a significant model in the F355 line that contributed to its popularity among Ferrari enthusiasts.

name: F355 Challenge 1995-1998
description: The Ferrari F355 Challenge, introduced in 1995, was specifically designed for the Ferrari Challenge race series. It was based on the F355 Berlinetta and featured a series of factory modifications to prepare it for competitive racing. Initially, the F355 Challenge models were essentially standard road cars equipped with a factory-to-dealer supplied kit valued at approximately $30,000. This kit included the essential adaptations for racing such as roll cage mounts and the removal of interior carpets to reduce weight. Over the years, Ferrari delivered the F355 Challenge with increasingly comprehensive factory-fitted racing components. By 1998, the vehicles were nearly complete race cars, requiring only minor finishing touches like the addition of a rear wing by the dealer. The modification kit for the F355 Challenge included a roll cage, racing bucket seats, safety harnesses, a fire extinguisher, an engine cut-off switch, manual radiator fan control with upgraded fans, a competition steering wheel, lightweight exhaust, a competition clutch, and 14-inch Brembo brakes borrowed from the F40. The car also featured 18-inch Speedline magnesium wheels, Pirelli racing slicks, solid suspension bushings, competition springs, brake cooling ducts, a lightened front bumper, and front and rear tow hooks. Midway through the 1995 season, a perforated rear grille was added to improve air flow through the engine bay. The F355 Challenge retained the same 3.5-liter V8 engine and the dimensions of the standard F355 Berlinetta but was only available with a 6-speed manual transmission. In total, 108 F355 Challenge cars were produced. Despite claims of over 300 Challenge conversions, only 28 of these were right-hand drive (RHD) models, modified by Ferrari UK and MHT, with a further 10 sold to other RHD markets. These RHD models are particularly rare and have become highly sought after in countries like Indonesia, Australia, Japan, and South Africa. Each F355 Challenge is identifiable by a specific "F355 Challenge" emblem located on the rear of the vehicle.

name: F355 Spider "Serie Fiorano" 1999
description: In 1999, Ferrari introduced the F355 Spider "Serie Fiorano," a limited-edition version of the F355 Spider with production initially set at 100 units but ultimately totaling 104. This special edition was developed to bring the standard F355 Spider closer to the performance levels of the Challenge models, incorporating several significant upgrades tailored for enhanced track capability. The Serie Fiorano edition included a "Competizione-derived Fiorano suspension pack" which featured a wider track, stiffer springs, and a thicker anti-roll bar. Additionally, it was equipped with drilled and ventilated brake discs coupled with competition brake pads and a steering rack sourced from the Competizione model. These enhancements were aimed at improving the handling and braking performance to make it more suitable for high-performance driving. Aesthetic and functional upgrades included Challenge rear grilles, enameled Scuderia Ferrari shields, and extensive use of carbon fiber for the center console, door sills, and paddle shifters. The steering wheel was covered in suede, further emphasizing the car's sporty intent. Of the 104 Serie Fiorano units produced, 100 were designated for the U.S. market. Among these, 74 were equipped with the F1 paddle-shift transmission and 26 with the traditional 6-speed manual gearbox. The additional four units consisted of three destined for Europe and one for South Africa. The American units featured a numbered plaque on the dashboard, commemorating their limited-edition status, while the European versions did not have this numbered plaque. The Serie Fiorano package represented one of the last iterations of the F355 model line, combining aesthetic refinement with technical enhancements to offer a more dynamic and sporty driving experience, closely aligning it with Ferrari’s racing heritage.

name: F355 GT3 1999
description: The Ferrari F355 GT3 was developed as a unique project by the Italian company Supertech, led by Franco Scapini, a former Formula 1 test driver. In the mid-1990s, Scapini and his team sought to enhance the F355 Challenge model, which was originally designed for the Ferrari Challenge series but found to be too tame for more competitive international racing. The standard F355 Challenge, based closely on the road car, lacked the competitive edge needed for more serious racing events and was not homologated beyond the Ferrari Challenge series. Scapini's modified F355, transformed into the GT3 variant, featured extensive modifications aimed at improving performance. The car, originally a 1997 Ferrari F355 Challenge, was completely stripped and rebuilt in Scapini’s workshop in Gallarate. Modifications included a lighter chassis, an upgraded aerodynamics package, revised suspension and brakes, and a racing clutch. The engine was also enhanced to deliver 430 horsepower at 9,200 rpm. The F355 GT3 made its mark in competitive racing. It was notably successful in the Italian GT Championship, where Scapini clinched the GT3 class win at Misano in 1999. The car also competed internationally, including memorable races at Magny Cours and Daytona. At the 24 Hours of Daytona, Scapini and his team managed a commendable 32nd overall and 12th in class in 1999, though they did not finish the race in 2000 due to electrical issues. Today, this F355 GT3 is preserved as a historic racing car, eligible for prestigious vintage racing events such as the Peter Auto Series, Masters Series, and Historic Daytona 24. The car is accompanied by a detailed history file that includes its original Italian registration documents, the vehicle log book, and various period articles and photographs, underlining its rich and storied racing heritage.

name: 360 Modena 1999-2005
description: The Ferrari 360 (Type F131) is a two-seater, mid-engine, rear-wheel drive sports car produced from 1999 to 2005. It succeeded the Ferrari F355 and was later replaced by the Ferrari F430. In collaboration with Alcoa, Ferrari developed a new all-aluminum space-frame chassis that was 40% stiffer and 28% lighter than the previous steel chassis, despite being larger in size. This change contributed to a reduction in overall weight and an improvement in acceleration, with the 0 to 100 km/h time reduced to 4.5 seconds. The car's design marked a shift from the sharp angles and flip-up headlights of the past, featuring a new V8 engine with a 3.6-litre capacity and a power output of 400 horsepower. The first model released was the 360 Modena, followed by the 360 Spider and the high-performance Challenge Stradale. The latter included enhancements like carbon ceramic brakes, track-tuned suspension, and a power increase for better track performance. The 360 Modena, named after Enzo Ferrari's birthplace, was produced until 2005.

name: 360 Spider 2001-2005
description: The Ferrari 360 Spider was introduced at the 2000 Geneva Motor Show. Designed with a convertible structure from the onset, the 360 Spider features enhancements to compensate for the loss of torsional rigidity typically associated with removing a coupe's roof. These enhancements include strengthened sills, a stiffened front floorpan, and a redesigned windscreen frame. Additional side reinforcements and a cross brace in front of the engine ensure the necessary dynamic rigidity and passenger safety is maintained through a strengthened windscreen frame and roll bars. The Spider's design is characterized by a curvilinear waistline, with fairings that suggest the start of a roof and stable roll bars integrated within these raised areas. The use of light aluminum construction keeps the Spider only 60 kg heavier than the coupe. It showcases the same 3.6 L V8 engine as the Modena, capable of producing 400 horsepower, visible under a glass engine cover. To adapt to the convertible design, the engine's intake manifolds were repositioned closer together in the engine compartment, facilitated by larger side air intakes. Performance-wise, the Spider's acceleration is slightly slower than the Modena due to the minor increase in weight, and the top speed is somewhat reduced. The electrically operated convertible top, which can be stowed away when not in use, is available in multiple colors including black, blue, grey, and beige.

name: 360 Barchetta 2000
description: The Ferrari 360 Barchetta is a unique one-off model based on the Ferrari 360 Spider, commissioned in 2000 by Gianni Agnelli as a wedding gift for Luca Cordero di Montezemolo, then chairman of Fiat and president of Ferrari. This special car retains many features of its base model, the 360 Spider, but with several distinctive modifications. The main alterations include the removal of the soft top system and roll bars, a different engine cover, and the installation of a visor instead of a full windshield to enhance airflow over the car. The performance specifications of the Barchetta mirror those of the Spider, maintaining the same engine capabilities. Aesthetically, the car stands out with its Argento Nurburgring exterior paint complemented by red pinstriping. The interior is finished in black leather with cream fabric, and includes fabric seats with matching stitching. Notably, the dashboard is embroidered with the words "360 Barchetta" and the car is equipped with a paddle-shift gearbox.

name: 360 Challenge Stradale 2003-2005
description: The Challenge Stradale represents a track-focused version of the Ferrari 360 Modena, inspired by the racing series 360 Modena Challenge and the track-oriented Ferrari F355 "Fiorano". This model underscores Ferrari's commitment to enhancing track performance through meticulous improvements in handling, braking, and weight reduction, essential characteristics for racing. Designed with 20% track and 80% road use in mind, the Challenge Stradale boasts a modest 25 horsepower increase over the Modena, achieving 0 to 100 km/h acceleration in just 4.1 seconds. Significant enhancements include an improved power-to-weight ratio, heightened throttle response, and better steering feedback. The integration of ceramic brakes from the Enzo and lighter components such as a sports exhaust system contribute to a 110 kg reduction in weight compared to the standard Modena, assuming all lightweight options are selected. These options include the removal of the radio, use of lexan door windows, and substitution of Alcantara for leather. Additional weight savings of 74 kg were achieved by using lighter bumpers, removing interior soundproofing, and standardizing carbon fiber seats. The Challenge Stradale also features Brembo carbon ceramic brakes as standard, which not only reduce the car's weight by 16 kg but also enhance handling by minimizing unsprung weight and eliminating brake fade. Special lightweight BBS wheels further contribute to performance efficiency. While optional features like a stereo system and glass side windows can add back approximately 30 kg, the car remains significantly lighter and more agile, emphasizing its race-bred pedigree.

name: 360 Challenge Stradale 'Cornes Edition' 2004
description: The 'Cornes Edition' was made to celebrate 25 years of Cornes being the official importer of Ferrari's in Japan. The stand out features of this limited edition are the white rear grill and white rev counter along with the carbon fuel filler cap and paddle shift. These cars were also fitted with carbon ceramic brakes and gold brake callipers and each one has Michael Schumacher`s signature on the door.

name: 360 Challenge 1999-2004
description: The Ferrari 360 Challenge, derived from the 360 Modena, was a track-oriented variant designed specifically for the Ferrari Challenge racing series. Unlike its road car counterpart, the 360 Challenge was only available with an electrohydraulic-actuated automated manual transmission. At its launch, Ferrari touted the car's improved performance capabilities, with acceleration from 0 to 100 km/h in just 3.9 seconds, a notable 0.6 seconds faster than the standard Modena. The car featured enhanced aerodynamics, upgraded Brembo brakes with gold-colored calipers, and larger two-piece floating discs, along with specialized ABS software from Bosch. A lighter exhaust system contributed to a slight power increase over the standard engine. Produced in limited numbers with less than 200 units, the 360 Challenge was sold exclusively as a race car, with ownership conditional on participation in the Ferrari Challenge race series. This model marked a departure from previous Challenge versions, like the F355, which were essentially road cars modified for track use. The 360 Challenge boasted substantial improvements in handling and weight reduction, allowing it to significantly outperform the road version despite similar engine power. The car's interior was completely reconfigured for racing, with the removal of the stereo, electric windows and locks, soundproofing, airbags, air conditioning, and even the handbrake. It was equipped with a single carbon-fibre racing seat, FIA-approved restraint harnesses, and a roll cage for increased safety. Additionally, a fire suppression system was installed. The instrument cluster was simplified to a monochrome LCD that displayed essential engine data. Racing dampers replaced the adaptive suspension of the road car, and the braking system was enhanced with larger brakes and additional cooling ducts to handle the rigors of racing.

name: 360 N-GT 1999-2004
description: The Ferrari 360 N-GT, a significantly tuned version of the 360 Challenge race car, was developed by Michelotto for the N-GT category in the FIA GT Championship. Recognized as the fastest iteration of the Ferrari 360, the engine of the N-GT, when unrestricted, generated over 547 horsepower, enabling top speeds around 310 km/h and acceleration from 0 to 97 km/h in approximately 3 seconds. This model marked the culmination of the collaboration between Ferrari and Michelotto, with its most recent notable performance being a victory at the 2011 Britcar Championship by SB Race Engineering. In 2002, the 360 N-GT competed in the Australian Nations Cup Championship, managed by Prancing Horse Racing and driven by Australian racer John Bowe. The car finished third in the championship and was entered in the 2002 Bathurst 24 Hour race at Mount Panorama Circuit. It achieved pole position through driver Brad Jones, but faced mechanical issues with oil pressure that led to two engine changes during the race, ultimately forcing its retirement. Bowe continued to compete in the 360 N-GT, finishing second in the 2003 Nations Cup Championship. The car's final race was at the 2003 Bathurst 24 Hour, driven by a team that included David Brabham and Andrea Montermini. After performing strongly, running in third for several hours, the car was retired on lap 287.

name: 360 GT 2001 - 2003
description: The Ferrari 360 GT, a race-oriented version of the 360 Modena, was developed by Ferrari's Corse Clienti department in partnership with Michelotto Automobili to compete in the FIA N-GT class. The model debuted in the 2001 FIA GT Championship season, with Team JMB Giesse successfully winning both the N-GT Cup for Drivers and the N-GT Cup for Teams. Produced from 2002 to 2004, Ferrari manufactured 20 units of the 360 GT, making them available to customers through their Corse Clienti department. The 3.6-litre V8 engine of the 360 GT was enhanced to deliver 436 horsepower, representing a significant boost compared to the 360 Challenge models. The 360 GT underwent extensive weight reduction measures beyond what was seen in the regular 360 Challenge versions. Efforts included lightening the wiring loom, which alone saved 7 kg, and removing non-essential components like air-conditioning brackets. The doors and front compartment lid were reconstructed using one-piece carbon fibre, contributing to a total weight reduction of 91 kg over the 360 Challenge, bringing the 360 GT's kerb weight down to 1,070 kg. To meet race regulations, ballast was added to adjust the car's weight to the mandated 1,100 kg limit.

name: 360 GTC 2003-2004
description: The Ferrari 360 GTC was developed by Ferrari's Corse Clienti department in collaboration with Michelotto Automobili, replacing the earlier 360 GT model for competition in the N-GT class. Introduced in 2004, the 360 GTC carried forward the successful features of the 360 GT, including a sequential six-speed gearbox and an enhanced Magneti Marelli electronics package. It was homologated anew by the FIA/ACO, incorporating aerodynamic elements from the Challenge Stradale such as the front bumper, side skirts, engine cover, and a modified rear end. This redesign, informed by wind tunnel research, significantly improved vertical downforce through a new rear wing system. Weighing in at 1,100 kg with ballast, the 360 GTC also marked an advancement in engine performance. It featured a 3.6 L V8 engine, refined to improve fuel consumption while increasing power output. Originally, the 360 GT produced 445 horsepower, but the GTC increased this to 472 horsepower, even with 30.8 mm air restrictors required for N-GT class racing. Without these restrictors, the engine's power potential rises to around 550 horsepower. In 2009, versions of the 360 GTC participated in international endurance races including events at Silverstone, Sebring, and Le Mans, driven by the Veloqx-Prodrive Racing team. These de-restricted, fully tuned versions of the GT-C demonstrated the model's capabilities on some of the world's most challenging circuits.

name: F430 2005
description: The Ferrari F430, produced by Italian manufacturer Ferrari from 2004 to 2009, served as the successor to the Ferrari 360. Unveiled at the 2004 Paris Motor Show, this sports car marked an evolution in design and performance, leading to its replacement by the 458 in 2009. The F430's design, a collaborative effort between Pininfarina and Frank Stephenson of Ferrari-Maserati, carried significant aerodynamic refinements from the 360. Although it maintained the same drag coefficient, the modifications significantly increased downforce. The F430 shared its basic structure with the 360, including the Alcoa Aluminium chassis, roofline, doors, and glass, but featured a distinctly different appearance that drew heavily on Ferrari's rich racing heritage. Notable design elements included the tail lights and engine cover vents from the Enzo, the Testarossa-style name inscription on the driver's side mirror, and large oval front bumper openings that echoed the Ferrari 156 "shark nose" model from the 1960s.

name: F430 Spider 2005
description: The F430 Spider, the convertible variant of the F430, debuted at the 2005 Geneva Motor Show and is Ferrari’s 21st road-going convertible. Pininfarina designed the car using aerodynamic simulations similar to those used in Formula 1. The Spider features a two-stage folding roof that neatly tucks away above the engine bay. Although it shares the same interior and performance characteristics as the coupé, the Spider is slightly heavier, which reduces its top speed by 5 km/h compared to its hardtop counterpart.

name: F430 Spider Bio Fuel 2008
description: The F430 Spider Bio Fuel, an ethanol-powered version of the F430 Spider, was showcased at the 2008 Detroit Auto Show. This model retains the standard 4.3-liter V8 engine, now producing 500 horsepower. It also achieves a 5% reduction in carbon dioxide emissions compared to the regular F430 Spider, aligning with more environmentally friendly performance standards.

name: SP1 2008
description: The SP1, Ferrari's newest supercar, marks the first vehicle designed under the company’s own customization program. Designed by Fioravanti and built by Ferrari, the SP1 offers a shift in how Ferrari approaches bespoke vehicles. Unlike previous models like the 612 Kappa, 575 GTZ, G 50, and P4/5 by Pininfarina, which were outsourced and privately upgraded, the SP1 is both designed and sold directly by Ferrari. Japanese collector Junichiro Hiramatsu recently added the SP1 to his extensive collection of Ferraris, which includes various models and literature. With the launch of the Portfolio Program, Ferrari aims to keep customization in-house and discourage external upgrades, despite the higher cost associated with their bespoke offerings.

name: 430 Scuderia 2008
description: The 430 Scuderia, unveiled by Michael Schumacher at the 2007 Frankfurt Auto Show, succeeds the 360 Challenge Stradale. Designed to rival high-performance cars like the Porsche 911 GT2 and the Lamborghini Gallardo Superleggera, the 430 Scuderia is lighter and more powerful than the standard F430, boasting a horsepower of 503 at 8,500 rpm. The increase in power is attributed to a revised intake, exhaust, and an ion-sensing knock-detection system that enables a higher engine compression ratio. These enhancements have improved the weight-to-power ratio from 2.96 kg/hp to 2.5 kg/hp. The Scuderia also features upgraded transmission software, "Superfast2," which allows for faster shift times of 60 milliseconds. It integrates a new traction control system that combines the F1-Trac system from the 599 GTB with stability control and the E-Diff electronic differential. The car can accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in 3.6 seconds and reaches a top speed of 319 km/h.

name: F430 Challenge 2006
description: The Ferrari F430 Challenge, unveiled at the 2005 Frankfurt Motor Show, is a race car developed from the road-going Ferrari F430, designed specifically for competition in the Ferrari Challenge and the Rolex Sports Car racing series. This vehicle builds on the legacy of its predecessor, the Ferrari 360 Challenge, by incorporating similar modifications that optimize it for track performance. Retaining the same 4.3L V8 engine as the standard F430, the F430 Challenge benefits from a number of enhancements that focus primarily on performance rather than major engine changes. These enhancements include a racing exhaust system and tweaks to the engine management system to improve efficiency and performance. Additionally, the engine undergoes fine blueprinting to ensure it operates at peak performance under race conditions. Significant weight reduction measures have been implemented in the F430 Challenge. These include the use of lighter materials and the removal of non-essential weight-contributing components that are typically found in the road car. The suspension system has also been extensively modified to better suit the rigors of track racing, offering improved handling and stability at high speeds. Stylistically, the F430 Challenge maintains much of the road car's aesthetics but incorporates specific changes tailored for racing. This includes the adoption of Formula One-inspired BBS wheels fitted with a single central nut, facilitating quicker tire changes during races. These wheels are paired with large carbon brakes, providing exceptional stopping power necessary for competitive racing. Overall, the F430 Challenge exemplifies Ferrari’s commitment to providing a high-performance race car that retains the DNA of its road-going versions, yet is perfectly adapted to the demands of motorsport environments.

name: F430 GTC 2006
description: The Ferrari F430 GTC, developed since 2006 by Ferrari's Corse Clienti department in collaboration with Michelotto, represents Ferrari's pinnacle in GT2 class racing technology, tailored specifically for competition in prestigious international series such as the American Le Mans Series, the Le Mans Series, and the FIA GT Championship. This model has also been a regular competitor at the iconic 24 Hours of Le Mans, asserting its position as the fastest and most advanced racing iteration of the F430 platform. In order to meet the strict regulations of the FIA GT2 championship, which aim to ensure parity among competing vehicles, adjustments are made to the cars including minimum weight requirements and the use of engine restrictors that vary based on engine displacement. To optimize its performance within these constraints, Ferrari modified the F430 GTC’s original 4.3-liter V8 engine to a smaller 4.0-liter displacement. This strategic adjustment allowed the F430 GTC to compete in the 3.8–4.0 L subclass within GT2, which benefits from a lower minimum weight limit of 1,100 kg (2,425 lbs). This engine downsizing naturally results in a slight reduction in power output, with the engine producing 445 PS (439 horsepower). However, the smaller engine allows for a 50 kg (110 lbs) increase in the car’s minimum weight under race regulations. Despite this, the overall reduction in vehicle weight helps maintain a favorable power-to-weight ratio, ensuring that the F430 GTC remains highly competitive in its class. This meticulous balancing of regulations and vehicle dynamics showcases Ferrari’s commitment to excelling in GT racing by optimizing every aspect of the car’s performance and compliance with racing standards.

name: F430 GT3 2006
description: The Ferrari F430 GT3, developed in 2006 by JMB Racing, is a racing variant specifically tailored for the FIA GT3 European Championship and other national GT championships like the British GT and FFSA GT. This model is based on the F430 Challenge but features several enhancements that differentiate it from both its sibling and the more GT2-spec racing cars. The F430 GT3 is equipped with the same 4.3-liter V8 engine found in the F430 Challenge. However, for GT3 competition, this engine has been tuned to produce a higher output of 550 horsepower, which surpasses even the GT2 version of the F430 in terms of raw power. This tuning makes the GT3 one of the most potent iterations of the F430 series in Ferrari's racing lineup. Despite its greater power, the F430 GT3 adheres to GT3 regulations which require a specific power-to-weight ratio. For the GT3, this ratio mandates that the vehicle weighs approximately 1,219 kg (2,687 lbs) in race trim (excluding the driver and fuel). This weight is about 119 kg (262 lbs) heavier than its GT2 counterpart. The increased weight, despite the higher power output, contributes to its relatively slower performance on the track compared to the GT2. This was notably evident during the 2007 Spa 24 Hours endurance race, where the GT3 spec car's best qualification time lagged around 8 seconds per lap slower than the GT2 spec vehicle. This performance disparity underscores the significant impact of vehicle dynamics and weight distribution in racing, where even substantial increases in power cannot always compensate for the drawbacks of added weight, particularly in a highly competitive setting like GT racing.

name: 430 GT3 Scuderia 2009
description: For the 2009 racing season, Kessel Racing developed a new car, the Scuderia GT3, envisioned as the successor to the highly successful Ferrari 430 GT3. This new car is based on the high-performance Ferrari Scuderia road car, and significant modifications were made within the limitations set by FIA GT3 regulations. The Scuderia GT3 aims to surpass the performance of the F430 GT, which notably won the 2007 FIA GT3 European Championship. The adaptation of the Scuderia into a GT3 racing car involves comprehensive tuning and configuration adjustments to ensure it meets the competitive standards required for approval and homologation by the FIA. The car is expected to receive a new homologation form from the FIA, pending approval. Interest in the Scuderia GT3 is strong among Ferrari racing teams, with Kessel Racing planning to continue its involvement in the FIA GT3 Championship using this newer model. They will be joined by JMB Racing and CRS Racing, the latter of which is expanding its participation in international GT racing. CRS Racing has also confirmed a two-car entry in the FIA GT Championship, indicating a significant commitment to Ferrari's racing efforts across different levels of GT competition.

name: SCG P4/5 Competizione 2009
description: In September 2009, Glickenhaus announced his intention to race a new version of the P4/5 in 2010 24 Hours Nürburgring. The car, called the SCG P4/5 Competizione, would not be a conversion of his road car but instead an entirely new car with a Ferrari chassis, VIN and drivetrain, made under Glickenhaus's own car brand Scuderia Cameron Glickenhaus. In May 2010 however, it was revealed that the Competizione would in fact be raced in 2011, based on a 430 Scuderia. It would be built to FIA GT2 standards and raced by Scuderia Cameron Glickenhaus in an Experimental Class under the direction of Paolo Garella, former Head of Special Projects at Pininfarina. Ferrari completely distanced itself from the SCG P4/5 Competizione project in 2011, refusing to sell the team parts for vital engine rebuilds between races. The car finished 39th in the 2011 24 Hours Nürburgring (after bursting into flames) and in 2012 won its class and finished 12th overall.

name: Scuderia Spider 16M 2009
description: The Ferrari Scuderia Spider 16M is a limited-edition marvel crafted to commemorate Ferrari's 16 Formula One Constructors' Championships. With only 499 units produced, it represents a rare blend of racing heritage and road-ready engineering. Based on the Ferrari Scuderia coupe, the 16M inherits the higher compression 4.3-liter V8 engine of the Scuderia, delivering an additional 20 bhp and 4 lb/ft of torque over the base Spider model. Significant enhancements were made across the board, including the body kit, aerodynamics, suspension, interior, and electronics, all fine-tuned to match the Scuderia coupe’s specifications. Weighing around 3300 pounds, the 16M sheds nearly 200 pounds compared to the F430 Spider, though it is still over 200 pounds heavier than the 430 Scuderia, largely due to its convertible nature. Performance tests at Ferrari’s Fiorano circuit proved the 16M to be faster than any other open-top road car previously produced by Ferrari. This isn't just about track performance; the 16M excellently balances high-octane sports car thrills with everyday usability. The adjustable suspension can be softened to provide a more comfortable ride, easing the intensity of the car's more performance-focused setup. Driving the 16M with the top down offers an exhilarating experience, thanks to the sonorous roar of its 503 horsepower V8 engine that revs up to 8,500 rpm. The sound is reminiscent of an F1 car, providing an unmatched auditory thrill—something that newer turbocharged models may struggle to replicate. The debut of the 16M was a highlight at the Ferrari Finale in Mugello, emphasizing its role as a celebration of Ferrari's continuous F1 success. Its exclusivity and limited production make it highly desirable, likely causing concern among those awaiting other models like the new California. Interestingly, the name "16M" is not only significant for its reference to Ferrari's racing achievements but also nods to the brand’s history, echoing the chassis number of one of Ferrari’s earliest production cars, the 166MM Touring Barchetta. This model helped secure Ferrari’s victory in the 1949 Mille Miglia, marking the beginning of Ferrari’s prominence in automotive racing and production.

name: MAT New Stratos 2018
description: The MAT New Stratos is often regarded as a superior Ferrari compared to current models sold by the brand. Built on the chassis of an older model, the F430, the New Stratos pays homage to the 1973-1978 Lancia Stratos. Despite its origins in the past, the New Stratos has carved out a niche as a true modern classic, developed to celebrate the iconic Lancia Stratos of rally fame. The design journey for the New Stratos began at the Bertone design house in Turin, with Marcello Gandini at the helm. Gandini, who had previously created landmarks like the Lamborghini Miura and Countach, also designed the Lancia Stratos Zero, a concept car that led to the roadgoing Stratos HF. This vehicle was designed specifically for rally, distinguishing it from other cars adapted for racing, and it excelled in the World Rally Championship during the mid-70s. Decades later, the Stratos name was resurrected by Chris Hrabalek, who bought the rights and created the Fenomenon Stratos as a student project. This effort eventually led to the New Stratos, which debuted as a prototype at the 2005 Geneva auto show. The project saw multiple potential partnerships with firms like Prodrive and Pininfarina, with the latter finally helping to push the car towards production. The modern Stratos utilizes a Ferrari 430 Scuderia as its base, maintaining the soul of a Ferrari with significant modifications. Despite challenges, including the unexpected death of Andrea Pininfarina, the car transitioned from prototype to production, albeit in a limited run due to parts acquisition issues with Ferrari. Physically, the New Stratos is distinctive, featuring a shortened wheelbase and a body crafted from carbon fiber. Its interior is spartan, reflecting its racing heritage, and the car lacks modern comforts in favor of pure driving pleasure. The engine, a mid-mounted 4.3-liter V-8, is tuned to deliver an estimated 540 horsepower, providing exceptional power-to-weight performance. Ultimately, the New Stratos is celebrated not for its suitability as an everyday car but for encapsulating the spirit of a true sports car. It strips down to the essentials, prioritizing the raw, exhilarating experience of driving over comfort, making it a worthy bearer of the Stratos legacy and a testament to what Ferrari once stood for in the automotive world.

name: 458 Italia 2009-2015
description: The Ferrari 458 Italia, designated as Type F142, is a mid-engine sports car produced by Ferrari and serves as the successor to the F430. It was first unveiled at the 2009 Frankfurt Motor Show and was later replaced by the 488 GTB in 2015. The 458 marked a significant evolution from its predecessor, incorporating an entirely new design influenced by Ferrari’s Formula One experience. The 458 features a 4.5-liter V8 engine from the "Ferrari/Maserati" F136 family. This engine delivers 570 PS (562 horsepower) at 9,000 rpm and generates 540 N⋅m (398 lb⋅ft) of torque at 6,000 rpm, with 80% of that torque available from 3,250 rpm. Notably, the 458 introduced direct fuel injection to Ferrari's mid-engine lineup, enhancing both performance and fuel efficiency. The car’s development included advanced technological contributions from Ferrari's long-standing partner, Magneti Marelli, particularly in the area of the body computer system. This integration of cutting-edge technology and engineering prowess makes the 458 Italia a standout example of Ferrari's commitment to pushing the boundaries of sports car performance.

name: 458 Italia China Edition 2012
description: The 2012 Ferrari 458 Italia China Edition is a special version created exclusively for the Chinese market to celebrate the 20th anniversary of Ferrari's presence in China. This edition is highly exclusive, with only 20 units produced, marking two decades since the first Ferrari, a 348 TS, was ordered in Beijing. This limited edition model features several unique design elements that pay homage to Chinese culture. It sports a vibrant Marco Polo Red body color and is adorned with a golden dragon graphic on the front bonnet, symbolizing power and good fortune. Complementing this are gold and black livery stripes that represent a racing track, further enhancing its sporty aesthetic. The car also includes gold-painted wheels and gold aeroelastic winglets, adding to its distinctive appearance. Inside, the attention to detail continues with gold embroidery on the headrests and an engine start button inscribed with the simplified Chinese characters for "start." Additionally, a "20th Anniversary Special Edition" plaque on the dash commemorates this special milestone. The 458 Italia China Edition was unveiled at the Italia Center of Shanghai World Expo Park, underlining Ferrari’s commitment to celebrating its significant market presence and cultural ties with China.

name: SP12 EC 2012
description: The Ferrari SP12 EC is a unique one-off sports car developed specifically for English musician Eric Clapton as part of Ferrari's Special Projects programme. This bespoke vehicle, based on the 458 Italia, was unveiled in May 2012 and later showcased at the 2013 Goodwood Festival of Speed. Designed by the Ferrari Styling Centre in collaboration with Pininfarina, the SP12 EC's exterior draws heavy inspiration from the Ferrari 512 BB, one of Clapton’s favorite Ferraris, blending classic design elements with modern aesthetics. The car retains the mechanical components of the 458 Italia, including its 4.5-litre V8 engine and the seven-speed dual-clutch transmission. However, many of the car’s technical details remain confidential, highlighting its exclusive nature. The project, which is reported to have cost £10,000,000, represents a high level of customization and personalization, underscoring Ferrari's commitment to catering to the individual tastes and desires of its distinguished clientele. The SP12 EC not only reflects Clapton’s admiration for the Ferrari brand but also showcases the possibilities within Ferrari’s custom car program to create highly personalized, bespoke automotive works of art.

name: 458 Spider 2011-2015
description: The Ferrari 458 Spider, the convertible counterpart to the 458 Italia, was unveiled at the 2011 Frankfurt Motor Show. This model features an innovative aluminum retractable hardtop, which not only weighs 25 kilograms less than the traditional soft top found on the previous Ferrari F430 Spider but also operates in just 14 seconds. To integrate this retractable roof system, the engine cover of the 458 Spider was re-engineered. Despite the additional mechanisms required for the retractable top, the 458 Spider maintains the same impressive acceleration as the 458 Italia, reaching 0–100 km/h in just 3.4 seconds. However, the Spider's top speed is slightly reduced to 320 km/h, a consequence of the slight increase in weight due to the convertible design. This model seamlessly blends the Italia's performance with the added luxury and aesthetic of a convertible, making it a desirable choice for those seeking the thrill of a high-performance sports car with the versatility of an open-top driving experience.

name: 458 Speciale 2013-2015
description: The Ferrari 458 Speciale, introduced at the 2013 Frankfurt Motor Show, is a high-performance variant of the 458 Italia, featuring numerous enhancements that distinguish it from the standard model. Key external upgrades include forged wheels, a vented bonnet, finned side sills, a taller rear spoiler, and redesigned bumpers. These changes are not just aesthetic; the car incorporates active aerodynamics developed by the Ferrari Styling Centre in collaboration with Pininfarina. These include movable flaps at the front and rear that dynamically adjust to balance downforce and reduce drag while the car is at speed. Under the hood, the Speciale's 4.5-liter V8 engine has been tuned to deliver 605 PS (597 horsepower) at 9,000 rpm and 540 N⋅m (398 lb-ft) of torque at 6,000 rpm. To harness this increased power, the electronic systems have been upgraded, including the introduction of a side slip angle control (SSC) system. This innovative feature works in conjunction with the F1-Trac traction control and the E-Diff electronic differential to optimize torque management and distribution, enhancing the car's handling at its performance limits. Performance-wise, the 458 Speciale can accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in just 3.0 seconds, and from 0 to 200 km/h in 9.1 seconds. It also boasts an impressive lap time of 1:23.5 on Ferrari’s Fiorano test track, which is just 0.5 seconds slower than the F12 berlinetta. The Speciale achieves a lateral acceleration of 1.33 g, reflecting its exceptional agility and cornering capabilities. Only 1,309 units of the Speciale were produced, making it a rare and sought-after model among Ferrari enthusiasts.

name: 458 Speciale A 2015
description: The Ferrari 458 Speciale A, a convertible version of the 458 Speciale, was introduced at the 2014 Paris Motor Show. The "A" in its name stands for "Aperta," which means "open" in Italian, highlighting its convertible design. This model was highly exclusive, with production limited to just 499 units, including 49 right-hand drive versions. Like its coupé counterpart, the Speciale A is powered by a 4.5-liter naturally aspirated V8 engine that produces 605 PS (597 horsepower) and 540 N⋅m (398 lb⋅ft) of torque. It matches the coupé's performance in acceleration, reaching 0–100 km/h in 3.0 seconds, and has a top speed of 320 km/h (199 mph). At the time of its release, it was the most powerful street-legal, naturally aspirated convertible ever produced by Ferrari. This record was held until the launch of the Ferrari LaFerrari Aperta in 2017, which features a 790 horsepower naturally aspirated V12 engine.

name: 458 MM Speciale 2016
description: Ferrari recently showcased its newest One-Off creation, the Ferrari 458 MM Speciale, during its inaugural shakedown at the Fiorano circuit with Ferrari's chief test driver Dario Benuzzi and the car's British owner behind the wheel. This unique vehicle was meticulously crafted by the Ferrari Styling Centre, using the chassis and running gear of the 458 Speciale as its foundation. The 458 MM Speciale was designed to fulfill the owner's desire for a highly sporty aesthetic, coupled with innovative features like the 'visor' effect for the glasshouse. This design includes a black-painted A-pillar reminiscent of the 1984 Ferrari GTO, creating a seamless glass surface that connects the windscreen and side windows, enhancing the car's aerodynamic silhouette and lowering the roofline. The car is painted in Bianco Italia and features a livery inspired by the Italian flag. Its bodywork is entirely new, handcrafted from aluminum with carbon-fibre bumpers at both ends. A standout feature is the new side air scoop, part of a broader revision of the car’s aerodynamics. This includes newly positioned coolant radiators and redefined side intakes that help cool the engine bay more efficiently due to their altered angle and positioning. Aerodynamic enhancements extend to the rear of the car, where a new spoiler not only augments downforce but also visually extends the beltline that flows from the front wheel arch, reinforcing the car’s dynamic stance. This model rides on bespoke wheels, specially designed for this unique build. Interior upgrades focus on luxury and personalization, with a high-quality audio system and refined trim tailored to the owner's preferences. The interior is finished in Cioccolato leather with white stitching, complemented by satin white rings around the tunnel controls, adding sophisticated touches that enhance the bespoke nature of the cabin. This one-off Ferrari 458 MM Speciale exemplifies Ferrari's commitment to custom tailored solutions, merging cutting-edge design with the technical underpinnings of one of its most celebrated models.

name: 458 Italia GT3 2011
description: In 2011, Ferrari introduced the 458 Italia GT3, developed by Michelotto, as a race-oriented version of their popular 458 Italia. This GT3 variant is lighter and more powerful than its GTE counterpart, producing about 558 PS (550 horsepower) with a higher redline of 9,000 rpm. The engine of the GT3 version bears more similarity to the 458 Italia road car than the GTE version, primarily due to different performance objectives and aero regulations. The aerodynamic design of the 458 Italia GT3 is tailored to meet GT3 class specifications, differing from the GTE in various aspects to comply with distinct regulatory requirements. These modifications helped enhance its performance on racetracks around the world, leading to an impressive record of victories and titles across several prestigious events and series. The car has an illustrious track record, including multiple wins at the 24 Hours of Spa-Francorchamps, victories in the Gulf 12 Hours, and significant achievements at the 12 Hours of Sepang, 24 Hours of Dubai, Liqui Moly Bathurst 12 Hour, and the 24 Hours of Barcelona. Beyond these, the 458 Italia GT3 has consistently performed well in series like the Blancpain Endurance Series, where it won several GT3 PRO AM Team and Drivers' titles and Gentlemen Trophy honors. In the European Le Mans Series, it clinched the GTC Team and Drivers' Titles in 2013 and 2014. The GT3 also demonstrated its dominance in the International GT Open and other regional series such as the GT3 Asia, showcasing Ferrari's engineering prowess in adapting a road car into a championship-winning race car. By 2015, the 458 Italia GT3 continued to compete vigorously across the globe, participating in numerous series including the Blancpain Endurance Series, European Le Mans Series, International GT Open, GT3 Asia, Pirelli World Challenge, and many national GT3 championships, underscoring its versatility and enduring competitive edge in the realm of GT racing.

name: 458 Italia GT2 2012
description: The Ferrari 458 Italia GT2, developed by Michelotto, was Ferrari’s answer to the competitive GTE class in ACO and FIA-sanctioned championships. Unveiled in 2011, the 458 Italia GT2 was tailored to meet the strict specifications of GTE racing, featuring significant alterations from the road-going version of the 458 Italia. Key changes in the GT2 included the removal of the "flex splitter" seen on the road cars, replaced with a more conventional inlet, with air exiting through louvers on the bonnet. This was part of broader aerodynamic optimizations tailored to GTE regulations. The engine, a 4.5-liter V8, was restricted under new regulations to produce 470 PS (460 horsepower), less than both the road version and the 458 Challenge variant. This engine was adapted to produce robust torque while having a lower redline at 6,250 rpm due to the restrictor requirements. The car retained paddle-shifting, allowed by amended rules from 2011, although the double-clutch gearbox was replaced. The 458 Italia GT2 proved highly successful in endurance racing, securing wins at prestigious events such as the 24 Hours of Le Mans in 2012 and 2014, the 12 Hours of Sebring in 2012, and the Petit Le Mans in both 2011 and 2012. It dominated various championship titles over the years, including the Intercontinental Le Mans Cup GTE Manufacturers' and the GTE PRO Team Titles in 2011. With the advent of the FIA World Endurance Championship in 2012, it continued to excel, capturing the GTE Manufacturers' and GTE PRO Team Titles multiple times and winning numerous other championships across different series. By the end of its competitive life in 2015, the 458 Italia GT2 had amassed a commendable record, demonstrating Ferrari's prowess in adapting its road vehicles to top-tier race cars. It was eventually succeeded by the Ferrari 488 GTE in 2016, marking the continuation of Ferrari’s innovative approach to endurance racing.

name: 458 Italia Grand Am 2012
description: In 2012, Ferrari introduced a specialized version of the 458 GT3 tailored for Grand-Am racing. This modified 458, designed to meet the specific regulations and demands of Grand-Am competition, features several notable adjustments from the standard GT3 model. The car maintains the same weight as the GT3 version but produces significantly less downforce due to the series' restrictions. Additionally, the engine in the Grand-Am variant is more heavily restricted, delivering a power output of 507 PS (500 horsepower) and a reduced redline of 8,000 rpm. Unlike the GT3, which utilizes a dual-clutch automatic transmission, the Grand-Am version is equipped with a conventional sequential manual transmission, aligning with the series' specifications. Safety enhancements include modifications to the roll cage to comply with the stricter safety regulations of Grand-Am racing. The car also does not feature traction control or ABS, which are typically standard in many other racing variants but are excluded here to adhere to the rules. The Ferrari 458 Italia Grand-Am made its debut at the 2012 24 Hours of Daytona. Throughout the racing season, the car proved its capabilities and competitiveness. A notable achievement was made by the AimAutosport.com team when drivers Jeff Segal and Emil Assentato clinched the Grand-Am Rolex GT championship following a second-place finish at Laguna Seca on September 9, 2012. This success highlighted the car's performance and reliability under the rigorous conditions of Grand-Am racing.

name: 488 GTB 2016
description: The Ferrari 488 (Type F142M) represents a pivotal chapter in Ferrari's storied history of mid-engine sports cars. Introduced as the successor to the Ferrari 458, the 488 marked a significant shift by adopting a turbocharged engine, a move not seen since the iconic Ferrari F40. It was later succeeded by the Ferrari F8. At the heart of the Ferrari 488 is a 3.9-litre twin-turbocharged V8 engine, a step down in displacement from the 458's naturally aspirated engine but a leap forward in power output. The engine is part of the F154 V8 engine family, renowned for its performance and advanced engineering. This engine is notable for its use of twin parallel ball-bearing twin-scroll turbochargers from IHI/Honeywell and two air-to-air intercoolers. The turbine wheels, crafted from a low-density TiAl alloy typically used in jet engines, are designed to minimize inertia and withstand the high temperatures generated by the turbochargers. This powerplant produces an impressive 670 PS (493 kW; 661 hp) at 8,000 rpm and 760 N⋅m (561 lb⋅ft) of torque at just 3,000 rpm. Such figures contribute to a specific power output of 171.7 PS per litre and a specific torque output of 194.8 N⋅m per litre, setting new benchmarks for Ferrari in terms of power density. The Ferrari 488 GTB swiftly garnered critical acclaim, being named "The Supercar of the Year 2015" by Top Gear magazine and "Best Driver's Car" in 2017 by Motor Trend. Further cementing its legacy, the 488 Pista variant was declared the 2019 Supercar of the Year by automotive personality Jeremy Clarkson. These accolades underscore the 488's significant impact on the supercar landscape, combining Ferrari's racing heritage with groundbreaking advancements in turbocharged performance technology.

name: 488 Spider 2016
description: The Ferrari 488 Spider is the convertible variant of the 488 GTB, featuring a retractable hardtop similar to its predecessor. Unveiled in July 2015 and debuting at the Frankfurt Motor Show in September 2015, the Spider shares the same 3.9-litre twin-turbocharged V8 engine as the GTB, producing 670 PS. Despite its convertible design, the 488 Spider weighs only 50 kg (110 lb) more than the GTB but is 10 kg (22 lb) lighter than the 458 Spider, its direct predecessor. The additional weight slightly affects its performance compared to the GTB, with the 0–100 km/h (0–62 mph) time remaining at 3.0 seconds, and the 0–200 km/h (0–124 mph) time slightly longer at 8.7 seconds. The top speed is slightly lower at 325 km/h (202 mph).

name: 488 GT3 2016
description: The Ferrari 488 GT3 made its global racing debut at the Albert Park Circuit in Melbourne during the second round of the 2016 Australian GT Championship, which was held as a support category to the 2016 Australian Grand Prix on March 17. The car was fielded by the Australian team Vicious Rumour Racing, with Italian driver Andrea Montermini and Danish driver Benny Simonsen sharing driving duties. Over the course of the event's four races, Montermini secured a fifth place in the first race and 14th in the third, while Simonsen achieved a second-place finish in the second race and sixth in the final race. In a subsequent notable performance, the 488 GT3 clinched a victory at the 2017 Liqui Moly Bathurst 12 Hour, driven by Maranello Motorsport. The team started from pole position and maintained a strong presence at the front of the pack throughout the race, never dropping out of the top five. This win marked Ferrari’s second victory at the Bathurst 12 Hour, with both triumphs (2014 and 2017) achieved by Maranello Motorsport, underscoring the 488 GT3's competitiveness and reliability in endurance racing.

name: 488 GTE 2016
description: The Ferrari 488 GTE is a grand tourer racing car developed by Ferrari's Competizioni GT unit, designed specifically for endurance racing. It succeeded the Ferrari 458 Italia GT racing car, utilizing the Ferrari 488 GTB as its foundation. The 488 GTE was constructed to meet the Automobile Club de l'Ouest/FIA LM GTE regulations, which were introduced for the 2016 racing season. This car participated in prestigious series such as the FIA World Endurance Championship, the European Le Mans Series, and the GTLM class of the IMSA SportsCar Championship. The 488 GTE made its competitive debut at the 2016 24 Hours of Daytona, fielded by the Scuderia Corsa and Risi Competizione teams. Following the introduction of the road-going Ferrari 488, Ferrari confirmed that a racing variant was imminent. In August 2015, spy photos from the ACI Vallelunga Circuit revealed a heavily camouflaged prototype in testing, showcasing significant modifications from the previous 458 Italia GT. The 488 GTE was officially unveiled later that year during the 2015 Finali Mondiali at Mugello Circuit, alongside its GT3 counterpart. At its launch, it was announced that the 488 GTE would carry over the engine from the 488 GTB and was capable of being configured to either LM GTE or Group GT3 specifications, highlighting its versatility and advanced engineering for racing applications.

name: J50 2016
description: The Ferrari J50 is a limited edition targa top sports car, a special iteration based on the 488 Spider, crafted to commemorate the 50th anniversary of Ferrari's market presence in Japan. Designed by the Ferrari Styling Centre led by Flavio Manzoni and produced by Maranello's Special Projects department, the J50 integrates distinct design elements that pay tribute to the iconic Ferrari sports cars of the 1970s and 1980s. Featuring a sleek, angular aesthetic, the J50 diverges from the 488 Spider primarily through its use of a two-piece carbon fiber targa top. Unlike the 488's retractable hardtop, the J50's targa top is manually removable and designed to be stowed neatly behind the seats, contributing to the car's classic yet modern silhouette. Additionally, the J50 boasts an enhanced power output, with the car's engine producing 681 hp, which is a 20 hp increase over the standard 488 Spider. Only ten units of the J50 were produced, making it an exceptionally rare example of Ferrari's commitment to celebrating its significant milestones through distinctive, highly customized vehicles that blend historical homage with advanced automotive technology and design.

name: 488 GTE Evo 2017
description: On November 13, 2017, Ferdinando Cannizzo, the technical coordinator for Ferrari Competizioni GT, announced that both the 488 GTE and GT3 models would be receiving Evolution kits. These kits were designed to enhance the cars' reliability and aerodynamic performance. The 488 GTE Evo underwent its initial shakedown test at Ferrari's Fiorano Circuit on March 30, 2018. Subsequent testing, including wind tunnel evaluations at the WindShear facility, led to the removal of the front dive-planes from the 488 GTE Evo, indicating significant aerodynamic adjustments. Cannizzo also hinted that the 488 GTE could continue to compete until 2021 with this second Evolution update, potentially delaying the development of a new model, thus extending the racing lifespan of the 488 GTE under the current evolution strategy.

name: 488 Challenge 2017
description: The Ferrari 488 Challenge was unveiled at the 2016 Ferrari World Finals in Daytona, marking a significant addition to Ferrari's international Challenge racing series for clients. This event coincided with the series' 25th anniversary in 2017, a series that began in 1992 and has featured over 1,000 races with more than 1,000 drivers across three continents. The Ferrari Challenge has established itself as a breeding ground for drivers aspiring to advance to higher levels of international GT and prototype championships, with many participants achieving success in prestigious competitions like Grand-Am, IMSA, the FIA World Endurance Championship, and the 24 Hours of Le Mans. The Ferrari 488 Challenge is notable for being the first turbocharged model in the Challenge series and is currently the most powerful, equipped with a 670 cv 3.9-litre V8 engine, an evolution of the power unit that won the 2016 International Engine of the Year Award. The car boasts significant enhancements in engine performance, aerodynamics, and chassis development. These advancements have led to a new lap record at Ferrari's Fiorano test track, where the 488 Challenge completed a lap in 1'15.5", one second faster than its predecessor, the 458 Challenge EVO. Key modifications to the 488 Challenge include specific engine mapping optimized for racing, shorter gear ratios, and a modified variable torque management system designed to encourage shifting at the optimal times for maximum acceleration. The F1 DCT transmission has been revised with a new racing shift strategy that allows the car to reach maximum revs in fourth gear from a standstill in just six seconds. These improvements are complemented by a reduction in the overall weight of the powertrain, enhancing the car’s racing efficacy.

name: SP38 (Deborah) 2018
description: The Ferrari SP38 Deborah is a one-off creation crafted by the Ferrari Styling Centre, led by Flavio Manzoni, based on the specifications of a discerning customer. Utilizing the mechanical framework of the Ferrari 488, the SP38 Deborah underwent an 18-month design and development process that included substantial input from the client. This unique model draws design inspiration from iconic Ferraris like the F40 and the 308, featuring a completely reimagined body that enhances its historical echoes with modern design elements. Distinctive to the SP38 Deborah are its narrow headlamps integrated into the front bumper, which also houses the daytime running lights, diverging from the 488’s larger standard air intakes. Instead, the SP38 features subtler air intakes near the rear side windows, seamlessly blending into the bodywork. The rear of the car boasts an integrated fixed wing and a new carbon fiber engine cover, designed with three slats to improve engine cooling—a nod to the F40's aesthetic. This is coupled with a newly designed set of tail lights and a modified exhaust system that further differentiates it from its 488 base. The car's bespoke nature is highlighted by its custom 'Deborah red' paintwork, a homage to the owner's name, showcasing various shades of red and exclusive new wheels specifically designed for this model. While the power output remains in line with the 488, specific performance metrics and the development cost remain undisclosed. The Ferrari SP38 Deborah was first delivered to the owner at Ferrari’s test track in Maranello, Italy, and had its public debut at the prestigious Concorso d'Eleganza Villa d'Este on May 26, 2018. This special model exemplifies Ferrari's ability to merge customer vision with their rich legacy of automotive design and performance.

name: P80/C 2018
description: The Ferrari P80/C represents a daring and sophisticated venture into reimagining the sports prototype concept, guided by the Ferrari Styling Centre under Flavio Manzoni. This project was driven by a distinctive collaboration between Ferrari’s engineering, aerodynamics teams, and a particularly discerning client, well-versed in Ferrari's heritage. Coming from a family deeply rooted in admiration for the Prancing Horse, the client's extensive knowledge and passion for Ferrari made him an ideal collaborator for this intricate initiative. The genesis of the P80/C was the client's desire to create a contemporary sports prototype that draws inspiration from seminal Ferrari models, notably the 330 P3/P4 and the 1966 Dino 206 S. This brief challenged the Ferrari Styling Centre to forge a vehicle that stands apart from any current offerings within Ferrari’s lineup, echoing the transformative designs of historical models that not only dominated the tracks but also significantly influenced the design of subsequent elegant road cars. The P80/C aims to merge the aesthetic and functional elements of its legendary predecessors with modern engineering to create a vehicle with a unique and authentic soul—a "Hero Car". It encapsulates the pioneering spirit of the original track cars like the Dino 206 S, which seamlessly transitioned into influencing road vehicle design, exemplified by its relationship to the Dino 206/246 GT. While both share a foundational Ferrari DNA, they exhibit distinct stylistic narratives: the racing heritage is evident in the P80/C’s bold, assertive lines, whereas the Dino road versions showcase more understated and refined styling. This blend of old and new encapsulates Ferrari’s ongoing commitment to pushing the boundaries of automotive design and performance.

name: 488 Pista Piloti Ferrari 2018
description: In 2017, Ferrari celebrated a significant achievement in motorsports by securing both the drivers and constructors titles in the LM GTE Pro class of the FIA World Endurance Championship. The triumphant vehicle was the Ferrari 488 GTE number 51, expertly handled by drivers Alessandro Pier Guidi and James Calado from the AF Corse team. To honor this victory, Ferrari introduced a special edition of the 488 Pista, aptly named the 488 Pista Piloti. This special edition was meticulously crafted by Ferrari's Tailor-Made department, which is responsible for the personalized customizations requested by clients. The 488 Pista Piloti incorporates various technical and aerodynamic features directly derived from the racing variants of the 488, ensuring that the vehicle not only commemorates a racing triumph but also embodies the performance and spirit of the track. Externally, the 488 Pista Piloti features a distinctive new livery highlighted by stripes in the green, white, and red of the Italian flag, paying homage to the livery of the race-winning 488 GTE. It also prominently displays the "PRO" tag, referencing the racing class in which the 488 GTE competed. The doors are adorned with the Italian flag colors at the bottom and can be customized with a race number chosen by the client. The launch version notably features the number 51, echoing the car used by the championship-winning drivers. Additionally, aesthetic enhancements include a matte black S-Duct and natural carbon fibre elements like the "dovetail" suspended rear wing and vent surrounds. Inside, the 488 Pista Piloti is extensively appointed with Alcantara, a material chosen to mirror the interior feel of a race car. This same material is used in a special technique to upholster the sports seats in a perforated style similar to that found in racing cars. The tricolor motif of the Italian flag continues inside, appearing in the seat upholstery, floor mats, and even the paddle shifters, adding a touch of national pride and racing heritage. The 488 Pista Piloti is available in a selection of colors that include Corsa red, Tour De France blue, Daytona black, and Nurburgring silver. Importantly, this model is exclusively available to Ferrari drivers, reinforcing its status as a tribute to their achievements on the world stage.

name: 488 Pista 2019
description: The Ferrari 488 Pista, unveiled at the Geneva Motor Show on March 6, 2018, represents a significant evolution of the 488 GTB, drawing extensively from Ferrari's racing experience with the 488 GTE and 488 Challenge. This model is designed for enhanced performance, incorporating several key modifications aimed at improving its track capabilities. The Pista is powered by an upgraded 3.9-litre twin-turbocharged V8 engine, now producing 710 horsepower. It includes enhancements like strengthened pistons and titanium connecting rods, all adapted from Ferrari's racing developments. The car's dual-clutch transmission has also been optimized for quicker gear shifts when in race mode. Significant aerodynamic adjustments have been made to increase the car's downforce by 20% compared to the 488 GTB. Notable features include front bumper ducts and a large hood vent that contribute to increased downforce, improving stability and handling at high speeds. Additionally, air intakes have been strategically repositioned to optimize airflow. The interior focuses on weight reduction, utilizing carbon fibre and Alcantara materials to shed about 91 kg compared to the GTB, contributing to the car's agility and performance. Optional 20-inch carbon fibre wheels further reduce weight, enhancing handling and acceleration dynamics. These improvements enable the Ferrari 488 Pista to accelerate from 0–100 km/h in 2.85 seconds and reach 200 km/h in 7.6 seconds, with a top speed of 340 km/h. The Pista also features advanced systems like the Side-slip Angle Control with magnetorheological suspension to refine its high-speed handling, ensuring it not only moves faster but also with greater precision on the track.

name: 488 Pista Spider 2019
description: The convertible version of the 488 Pista, known as the Pista Spider, was introduced at the 2018 Pebble Beach Concours d'Elegance. It shares the same engine and power output as its coupé counterpart, resulting in similar performance levels, but it stands out by being lighter, weighing in at 1,380 kg. This distinction makes it the first production Ferrari convertible to be lighter than the coupé version it's based on. The Pista Spider's interior emphasizes its track-ready credentials, featuring extensive use of carbon fiber and Alcantara and leather upholstery. Instead of traditional door handles, the car uses straps to close the doors, underscoring its performance focus. Externally, the Pista Spider is equipped with new 20-inch diamond finish alloy wheels that are 20% lighter than standard forged alloy wheels, with an option for 20-inch carbon fiber wheels for further weight reduction. Additionally, the Pista Spider introduces the Ferrari Dynamic Enhancer, an advanced lateral dynamics control system designed to make driving at the limit more intuitive, controllable, and predictable. This feature enhances the driving experience, particularly in high-performance scenarios, making the Pista Spider a remarkable example of Ferrari's commitment to innovation in driving dynamics.

name: 488 GT Modificata 2020
description: The Ferrari 488 GT Modificata is a track-only limited edition car that blends advanced technologies from the 488 GT3 and 488 GTE. Designed to exceed the typical restrictions of technical and sporting regulations, it fully unleashes the potential of these racing platforms. Exclusive to track days and Ferrari Club Competizioni GT events, the 488 GT Modificata features an engine that produces up to 690 hp, which is 99 hp more than its regulated counterparts, enhancing its performance on the track.

name: 488 GT3 Evo 2020
description: In 2020, Ferrari introduced an updated version of their racing car, the 488 GT3 EVO 2020, which featured significant improvements in several key areas. These enhancements included a more aerodynamically efficient front end, refined vehicle control systems, and better ergonomics within the cockpit. A notable upgrade was the incorporation of a new, lighter racing seat developed in collaboration with Sabelt, aimed at improving driver comfort and vehicle performance. The engine of the 488 GT3 EVO 2020, while subject to racing restrictions, is capable of producing approximately 600 PS (441 kW; 592 hp) and 700 N⋅m (516 lbf⋅ft) of torque. Building on these improvements, the Ferrari 488 GT3 Evo achieved a significant milestone in 2021 by winning the PRO Class category at the 24 Hours of Spa. The car was entered by the Iron Lynx Motorsports team, and this victory underscored the effectiveness of the evolutionary changes made to the 488 GT3 platform, marking it as a formidable contender in endurance racing circuits.

name: 488 Challenge Evo 2020
description: The Ferrari 488 Challenge Evo debuted at the 2019 Ferrari Finali Mondiali at the Mugello Circuit, showcasing significant improvements three years after the original 488 Challenge was introduced at Daytona.
